While every effort has been made to ensure the accuracy of the technical instructions, cloud providers frequently update their consoles and CLI tools.

Students are responsible for monitoring their cloud spend and ensuring that resources are decommissioned after labs are completed.

Preface: The Shift to Enterprise Scale

In the early stages of cloud adoption, a single-account setup is often sufficient. It provides a quick playground for innovation and a straightforward path to deployment. However, as organizations grow, this "flat" architecture quickly becomes a liability. The risks of a single-account setup—ranging from a massive blast radius during a security breach to hitting rigid API rate limits—create a glass ceiling for scalability.

The Landing Zone Specialist curriculum was designed to shatter that ceiling. This textbook is not just about learning cloud services; it is about mastering Governance as Code. We move beyond simple resource creation to focus on the skeletal system of the enterprise cloud: identity hierarchies, service control policies (SCPs), and hub-and-spoke networking architectures.

Why This Course?

The modern Cloud Engineer must be an architect who thinks in terms of "Account Factories" and automated guardrails. Over the next 14 weeks, you will transition from manual configurations to deploying standardized environments at scale using tools like Terraform and AWS Account Factory (AFT).

Our goal at Hexadigitall Technologies is to equip you with the mental models and hands-on experience required to lead a corporate handover. You aren't just building a cloud; you are building a secure, automated, and sustainable landing zone for the future of business.

"Scale is not an accident; it is a design choice."

Hexadigitall Technologies — Advanced Infrastructure Series

The Hexadigitall Methodology: Engineering Your Career

This textbook is not just a collection of tutorials; it is a 14-week Proof of Work system. Every page is engineered using a tiered pedagogical approach based on Bloom’s Taxonomy, ensuring you don't just "follow" instructions, but learn to own the architecture.

1. Foundations (Understand)

Pre-class exercises that prime your mental models. You start every week by setting up a dedicated folder in your GitHub repo for the upcoming architectural theme.

2. Teaching Notes (Analyze)

Deep-dives into the "Why." We analyze the trade-offs of SCP hierarchies and Hub-and-Spoke routing so you can defend your choices in a professional setting.

3. Hands-on Labs (Apply)

The "How." Deploying production-grade, multi-account infrastructure using Terraform and Cloud CLI. These labs result in clean, reusable code snippets.

4. Structured Projects (Create)

The "Proof." Independent builds designed for portfolio scaling. By the end of each week, you push an audited project folder to GitHub.

Accelerating Your Growth: Mentorship & Mastery

Architecting Landing Zones is a high-stakes endeavor. For those seeking to bridge the gap between "knowing" and "doing," we offer two distinct pathways to professional mastery:

  • Pathway A: Modular Mentorship (Monthly)

    Perfect for self-paced readers. Subscribe for the months you need support. Access a mentor to guide you through pre-class exercises, audit your GitHub pushes, and practice Architectural Defense—learning to justify your designs for high-level technical interviews. We monitor your progress and help you package your 14-week commit history as a world-class portfolio.

    Explore Mentorship Options →
  • Pathway B: The Full Course Experience

    Includes the textbook, full mentorship, and live cohort-based tutoring. This is for those who want a structured classroom environment, deep-dive peer reviews, and direct tutelage from the Hexadigitall engineering team.

    Enroll in the Next Cohort →

Portfolio Tip: Recruiters value consistency. By the time you finish this textbook, your GitHub will show 14 distinct folders of enterprise-level work—creating a chronological audit trail of your expertise that outshines any traditional resume.

Hexadigitall Technologies — Deploying the Future of Enterprise Cloud.

Entry Behavior & Prerequisites

This is a Specialist Level curriculum. To ensure success in architecting enterprise Landing Zones, students must move beyond basic resource deployment and possess a firm grasp of cloud-native infrastructure patterns.

Technical Baseline

  • • Intermediate Terraform: You should be comfortable with modules, state files, and variables. This course will advance those skills into multi-account state management and Account Factory automation.
  • • Cloud Competency: Experience with at least one major provider (AWS or Azure) at an Associate or Professional level. Specifically, familiarity with IAM, VPCs, and basic account-level security.
  • • Git Workflow: Proficiency in branching, committing, and pushing code. Your GitHub repository will serve as the primary audit trail for your mentorship and portfolio.

Environmental Readiness

Before beginning Week 1, your local workstation must be configured with the following:

- Terraform CLI (v1.5.0+)
- AWS CLI or Azure CLI
- Git (configured with SSH/GPG for signed commits)
- VS Code (Recommended extensions: HashiCorp Terraform)

Portfolio Architecture

We recommend creating a clean GitHub repository titled landing-zone-specialist-portfolio. Throughout this textbook, you will be instructed to create weekly folders (e.g., /week-01-foundations, /week-04-networking). This structure is essential for the monthly mentorship reviews and project defenses.

Account Warning: You will require administrative access to a Master/Root account within a Cloud Organization. Be advised that certain Landing Zone services (e.g., Transit Gateway, Control Tower) are not covered by the "Free Tier."

Includes: hub-and-spoke topology, transit gateway routing, segmentation patterns, and hybrid readiness.

Week 5: Shared Services, Private Link & Secure Connectivity

Includes: secure shared service design, private connectivity patterns, service catalog integration, and edge trust boundaries.

Week 6: Automated Security & Detection Engineering

Includes: centralized security telemetry, guard duty automation, detection engineering, and response readiness.

Week 7: Observability, Logging & Audit Pipeline

Includes: telemetry architecture, log centralization, audit collection, and monitoring dashboard design.

Phase III: Automation, Delivery & Platform Governance

Week 8: Automated Account Factory & Self-Service Provisioning

Includes: account vending automation, secure IaC pattern, environment promotion, and state governance.

Week 9: Account Customizations, Post-Deployment Controls & Drift Guardrails

Includes: custom account policies, post-deployment lifecycle controls, drift detection, and remediation workflow.

Week 10: Platform Delivery with AFT & Terraform Automation

Includes: delivery pipelines, change management, gated releases, and deployment safety.

Week 11: Standardization, Blueprints & Service Catalog Onboarding

Includes: platform standardization, blueprint governance, workload onboarding playbooks, and service catalog patterns.

Phase IV: Lifecycle, Scale & Capstone Delivery

Week 12: Compliance Validation, Audit Evidence & Lifecycle Guardrails

Includes: compliance evidence, control mapping, audit readiness, and operational lifecycle guardrails.

Week 13: State Management, Scale Strategy & Resilience Planning

Includes: state management at scale, environment drift strategy, recovery planning, and resilience readiness.

Week 14: Capstone Delivery, Executive Handover & Architecture Defense

Includes: final architecture dossier, executive handover package, portfolio presentation, and defense preparation.

Course Prep

Course Preparation & Mentorship Guide

This week establishes the mentor-led structure, the portfolio repository rules, and the weekly artifact workflow that will make every later chapter a professionally packaged architecture delivery.

"A professional architecture engagement begins with the process just as much as the design. This week, we build the process."
Preparation

Course Preparation & Mentorship Guide

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Orientation: Mentor review cadence, artifact expectations, and weekly check-in structure.
  • 2. Setup: Portfolio repo structure, weekly folder naming, and Git commit hygiene.
  • 3. Deliverables: README, course roadmap, and mentor-ready presentation plan.
  • 4. Checkpoint: First mentor review, feedback loop, and defense preparation plan.

The value of this course is not only in the technical build; it is also in the habit of delivering polished, reviewable artifacts every week. This week sets the standard.

"A well-structured portfolio is the best proof that you can do the work again, and better."
COURSE PREPARATION & MENTORSHIP GUIDE
ORIENTATION

Orientation & Mentorship Workflow

This textbook is organized as a mentor-guided delivery cycle. Each week you will produce reviewable artifacts, submit them to your mentor, and use feedback to improve the next week's architectural work.

How to Use This Textbook

Start each chapter by reading the outcome summary and definition of the weekly deliverable. Then complete the repository action, document your reasoning, and update your portfolio folder. Treat the chapter as a professional engagement, not a lab assignment.

Weekly Mentor Review Cycle

Each week follows the same rhythm: prepare the artifact, submit it into the portfolio repo, share it with your mentor, receive feedback, and revise. This cadence is designed to build discipline and make your progress visible.

  • 1. Prepare: Complete the weekly analysis, architecture artifact, and supporting documentation.
  • 2. Submit: Push the work to the portfolio repo and notify your mentor with a clear summary of what changed.
  • 3. Review: Mentor evaluates the work, scores it, and provides constructive guidance.
  • 4. Revise: Implement feedback, refine the narrative, and document the decision rationale in the next weekly folder.

Portfolio Repository Rules

  • - Repo Name: Use landing-zone-specialist-portfolio as the canonical repository for this course.
  • - Folder Structure: Create weekly folders such as /week-01-foundations, /week-02-identity, and inside each include analysis.md, architecture.md, and supporting artifacts.
  • - Deliverables: Each week should include a README or summary document, a design artifact, and evidence of the implementation decisions.
  • - Git Hygiene: Use consistent commit messages, one logical change per commit, and preserve a clean history that demonstrates progression.

Deliverable Checklist Per Week

  • - Analysis: A problem statement, success criteria, and risk summary.
  • - Architecture: Diagrams, account design, and policy decisions.
  • - Code or Configuration: Terraform, CLI scripts, or architecture-as-code assets.
  • - Review Notes: Mentor questions, feedback status, and the next step plan.
  • - Executive Summary: A concise portfolio statement that explains what was built and why it matters.

How Your Mentor Scores Each Week

Mentor scoring is based on the professionalism, clarity, and completeness of your weekly delivery. These are the core dimensions:

  • - Clarity: Can a reviewer quickly understand the problem, approach, and result?
  • - Accuracy: Are the architectural concepts and implementation details correct?
  • - Completeness: Are the expected artifacts present and organized?
  • - Narrative: Does the work tell a coherent story that fits the enterprise use case?
  • - Professionalism: Is the deliverable polished, documented, and ready for a decision-maker?

How This Course Prepares You for Interviews & Enterprise Proposals

Every weekly portfolio artifact is also a story you can use in architecture interviews and proposal work. This course teaches you to document tradeoffs, justify decisions, and present a coherent enterprise solution—not just to build infrastructure.

Orientation Deliverables

  • - Portfolio Repository: A GitHub repo named landing-zone-specialist-portfolio with weekly folders and a polished README.
  • - Weekly Review Cycle: A clear mentor submission process for feedback, review, and revision.
  • - Process Guide: A written expectation summary for mentor handoff and score criteria.
  • - Career Narrative: An introduction that explains how this portfolio demonstrates enterprise architecture readiness.

This section is intentionally about the way we work, not the minute details of your CLI environment. The technical readiness checklist remains in the front matter, while this guide explains how to turn the course into a mentor-reviewed portfolio.

Mentor Note: This guide is your operating model. Your mentor will score the week on the quality of the outcome, the structure of the artifact, and your ability to translate technical work into enterprise value.

Phase I

Foundations &
Multi-Account Theory

An exploration into the architectural pivot from single-account environments to scalable, governed enterprise cloud structures. We establish the "Why" before we build the "How."

"Architecture is the art of how to waste space beautifully; in the cloud, it is the art of how to manage scale securely."
Week 01

Landing Zone Strategy & Blast Radius Control

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Entry Behavior & Blast Radius Theory.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: Cloud Provider Service Quotas & API Rate Limits.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Organization Health Audit via CLI.
  • 4. Weekly Project: Portfolio Initialization & Baseline Documentation.

Before deploying a single resource, we must understand the constraints of the cloud. This week focuses on the physical and logical limits that necessitate a multi-account strategy, while also establishing the mentor-ready portfolio, weekly documentation rhythm, and baseline deliverables your mentor will review.

"The first step in building a resilient system is acknowledging that everything fails eventually. The second step is making sure it doesn't matter when it does."
WEEK 01: LANDING ZONE STRATEGY & BLAST RADIUS CONTROL
FOUNDATION

Chapter 1: The Blast Radius Theory

In a traditional single-account cloud environment, every resource shares the same logical boundary. This shared boundary is known as the Blast Radius. If credentials are compromised, the entire organization is at risk. Architecting a Landing Zone is primarily an exercise in Blast Radius Reduction.

The Case for Account-Level Segregation

Architecting a Landing Zone is primarily an exercise in Blast Radius Reduction. By moving to a multi-account strategy, we ensure that:

  • 01. Security Isolation: A compromise in a "Sandbox" account cannot move laterally into the "Production" account.
  • 02. Quotas & Rate Limits: Heavy workloads in one account won't cause "Throttling" errors for critical services in another.
  • 03. Billing Transparency: Costs are naturally aggregated by account, making chargebacks to different departments seamless.

Architect's Note: When we build the Organization hierarchy in Week 2, our primary goal will be to design OUs (Organizational Units) that mirror these blast radius requirements.

Pre-Class Exercises: Initializing the Proof of Work

To support the 14-week "Proof of Work" cycle, you must initialize your local and remote environments. Complete these exercises before moving to the Teaching Notes:

  • Ex 1. Repository Creation: Create a new GitHub repository titled landing-zone-specialist-portfolio. Initialize it with a README.md and a .gitignore specifically for Terraform.
  • Ex 2. Directory Versioning: Create your first structural folder: /week-01-foundations. Inside, create a subfolder named /exercises.
  • Ex 3. CLI Connectivity: Authenticate your Cloud CLI (AWS/Azure) and run a command to list your current account ID. Redirect this output to a file named account_info.txt inside your week 1 exercises folder.

Mentorship Note: If you are enrolled in the Modular Mentorship, your mentor will audit these first commits to ensure your Git signature and folder structure meet enterprise standards.

WEEK 01: LANDING ZONE STRATEGY & BLAST RADIUS CONTROL
TEACHING NOTES

The Glass Ceiling: Service Quotas & API Throttling

Security is the most common reason for a multi-account strategy, but operational physics is often the most urgent. Every cloud account has invisible "hard" and "soft" limits that, once hit, can bring an entire enterprise to a standstill.

1. Service Quotas (Resource Limits)

Cloud providers limit the number of resources (VPCs, EC2 instances, IGWs) you can create per region per account. While some are adjustable, they represent a shared pool. In a single-account setup, a developer in "Dev" could accidentally consume all available Elastic IPs, preventing "Production" from scaling during a traffic spike.

2. API Rate Limits (The Throttling Problem)

Every time you run a terraform plan or a script calls DescribeInstances, you consume API credits. Cloud providers throttle these requests to protect their control plane.

Scenario: If you have 50 microservices in one account all being managed by a CI/CD pipeline, the sheer volume of API calls can trigger RequestLimitExceeded errors. This "noisy neighbor" effect can block critical security patches from deploying.

The Landing Zone Solution

By distributing workloads across multiple accounts, we effectively multiply our API quota and resource limits by the number of accounts in the Organization. This allows the enterprise to grow horizontally without ever hitting the "Glass Ceiling" of a single-account's constraints.

WEEK 01: LANDING ZONE STRATEGY & BLAST RADIUS CONTROL
HANDS-ON LAB

Lab 1.1: Auditing Organization Health

In this lab, you will use the Command Line Interface (CLI) to identify the current limits of your account. This data provides the baseline evidence needed to architect a multi-account structure.

Task 1: Identifying Account Constraints

Execute the following command to view your current service quotas for VPCs. This identifies how many "spoke" networks you can create before hitting a hard limit.

# AWS Example: List VPC Quotas
aws service-quotas get-service-quota \
  --service-code vpc \
  --quota-code L-F678F1CE

Task 2: Detecting "Noisy Neighbors"

Review your CloudTrail logs to identify which IAM entities are making the most frequent API calls. This exercise simulates identifying an application that might trigger API throttling for the whole account.

Lab Requirements

  • [ ] Capture a screenshot of your VPC quota output.
  • [ ] Identify one "Adjustable" quota vs one "Non-adjustable" quota.
  • [ ] Save these findings into your /week-01-foundations/exercises folder.

Architectural Defense: Why would an enterprise prefer to open a second account rather than simply requesting a quota increase for an existing one? Prepare to answer this in your project defense.

WEEK 01: LANDING ZONE STRATEGY & BLAST RADIUS CONTROL
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 1: Portfolio Initialization & Baseline Documentation

The objective of this project is to establish the Infrastructure as Code (IaC) standards for your 14-week portfolio and document the "Pre-Landing Zone" state of your environment.

Project Deliverables

1. Standardized Repository Structure

Finalize your GitHub structure. Your repository must now include a top-level docs/ folder and a projects/ folder. Week 1 findings should be migrated into projects/week-01-foundations/.

2. The "Blast Radius" Audit Report

Create a Markdown file titled ENVIRONMENT_AUDIT.md. Using the data from Lab 1.1, document at least three service quotas that would act as blockers for an enterprise scaling to 50+ VPCs in a single account.

3. Portfolio README Enhancement

Update your main README.md to reflect the "Landing Zone Specialist" mission. Include a "Tech Stack" section listing Terraform, Cloud CLI, and Git.

Submission Checklist

Signed Commits Are all commits GPG or SSH signed?
Folder Hierarchy Is the naming convention consistent (kebab-case)?
Documentation Does the Audit Report include raw CLI output snippets?

Career Strategy: This project is your first "Commit." It demonstrates to future employers that you possess the administrative discipline required to audit existing infrastructure before attempting to migrate it.

Week 02

Identity & Management Plane Architecture

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Hierarchical Management & OU Design Patterns.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: AWS Organizations vs. Azure Management Groups.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Provisioning Multi-Account Structures with Terraform.
  • 4. Weekly Project: Building the "Golden" Organizational Hierarchy.

Having established the necessity of multiple accounts, we now focus on the management plane. This week introduces the skeletal structure of the enterprise cloud: Organizational Units (OUs). We will learn to design hierarchies that balance security isolation with operational ease, setting the stage for global governance.

"Structure is not a constraint on creativity; it is the foundation that allows creativity to scale without collapsing into chaos."
WEEK 02: IDENTITY AT SCALE
FOUNDATION

Chapter 2: Hierarchical Management Patterns

Once an enterprise moves beyond a single account, the primary challenge shifts from resource management to governance management. We solve this by implementing a hierarchical structure using Organizational Units (OUs). An OU is a logical container for accounts that allows you to apply policies to multiple entities simultaneously, ensuring uniform security standards.

The Lifecycle-Based vs. Functional Hierarchy

There are two primary patterns for designing your Landing Zone hierarchy:

  • P1. Lifecycle Pattern: Organizing OUs by environment (e.g., Development, Staging, Production). This is ideal for applying strict security guardrails that only trigger in production.
  • P2. Functional Pattern: Organizing OUs by business unit or project (e.g., Finance, Engineering, Marketing). This is preferred for complex organizations with decentralized IT budgets.

Pre-Class Exercises: Scaling the Portfolio

To prepare for the automated deployment of your Organization, complete these structural tasks:

  • Ex 1. Portfolio Expansion: Create a new directory /week-02-identity in your repository. Inside, create subfolders for /exercises and /lab-code.
  • Ex 2. Documentation: In a file titled HIERARCHY_DESIGN.md, sketch a 3-level hierarchy for a fictional company with "Security," "Core," and "Workloads" OUs.
  • Ex 3. State Prep: Initialize a new Terraform configuration file in your /lab-code folder. Configure the provider to target your cloud organization's management account.

Architect's Note: The choices you make in your hierarchy design this week will dictate where you apply Service Control Policies (SCPs) in Week 3. Design for long-term stability.

WEEK 02: IDENTITY AT SCALE
TEACHING NOTES

The Management Plane: AWS vs. Azure

To build a truly cross-platform Landing Zone, an architect must understand how different providers handle account grouping. While AWS uses Organizations, Azure utilizes Management Groups. Both structures allow for policy inheritance, but their implementation logic varies.

1. AWS Organizations (OUs)

In AWS, the Management Account sits at the top. It creates Organizational Units (OUs), which are containers for accounts. A key constraint is that an AWS account can only belong to exactly one OU at a time. Policies (SCPs) applied to an OU are inherited by all child OUs and accounts beneath it.

2. Azure Management Groups

Azure takes a slightly more flexible approach. Management Groups sit above Subscriptions (Azure's equivalent of an AWS account). Unlike AWS, Azure allows for a deep nesting of up to six levels of Management Groups, providing a more granular control over complex corporate hierarchies.

Feature AWS Azure
Root Container Organization Root Root Management Group
Grouping Unit Organizational Unit (OU) Management Group
Individual Unit Account Subscription

Key Strategy: Regardless of the provider, always avoid placing accounts or subscriptions directly in the "Root." Always create at least one layer of management containers to prevent accidental global policy lockouts.

WEEK 02: IDENTITY AT SCALE
HANDS-ON LAB

Lab 2.1: Provisioning Multi-Account Structures

This lab focuses on automating the creation of Organizational Units (OUs) using Terraform. By the end of this session, you will have a live, version-controlled hierarchy within your Cloud Organization.

Task 1: Define the Root and Child OUs

Create a main.tf file in your /week-02-identity/lab-code directory. Use the snippet below as a template to define your first two management layers.

resource "aws_organizations_organizational_unit" "security" { name = "Security" parent_id = data.aws_organizations_organization.org.roots[0].id } resource "aws_organizations_organizational_unit" "workloads" { name = "Workloads" parent_id = data.aws_organizations_organization.org.roots[0].id }

Task 2: Deployment and Validation

Run the following Terraform commands. Ensure your CLI is authenticated with the Management Account of your Organization.

  • [ ] terraform init - Initialize the backend and providers.
  • [ ] terraform apply - Execute the plan to create the OUs.
  • [ ] Verify the new structure in the Cloud Console or via CLI: aws organizations list-roots.

Architectural Defense: If you delete the Terraform state file, what happens to the OUs in your cloud account? Understanding the difference between Resource Management and State Management is key for this week's project.

WEEK 02: IDENTITY AT SCALE
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 2: Building the "Golden" Organizational Hierarchy

The objective of this project is to deploy a multi-layered Organizational Unit (OU) structure that separates core infrastructure from business workloads. This "Golden" hierarchy is the industry standard for minimizing risk across the enterprise.

Project Deliverables

1. The Nested OU Configuration

Extend your Terraform code to include nested OUs. You must create a "Workloads" parent OU with "Development" and "Production" as child OUs. Ensure all resource dependencies are correctly mapped in your IaC.

2. Hierarchy Visualization Map

Create a file named ORGANIZATION_MAP.md in your /week-02-identity folder. Provide a visual tree (using ASCII or a diagram tool) that maps out your final root, OUs, and child OUs.

3. Scalability Strategy Write-up

Document why you chose a Lifecycle-based vs. Functional-based hierarchy. Explain how this structure prevents "Production" resources from being affected by "Development" mistakes.

Submission Checklist

IaC Versioning Is your Terraform code pushed to the /lab-code folder?
Resource Integrity Do your OUs follow a consistent naming convention?
Commit History Does your GitHub history show the evolution from Week 1 to Week 2?

Career Strategy: In professional interviews, you will be asked: "How do you organize 500 accounts?" Pointing to this project demonstrates that you don't just know the tools—you know the architecture of scale.

Week 03

Governance, Guardrails & Secure Access Controls

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Least Privilege & Global Guardrail Theory.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: SCPs vs. Permission Boundaries vs. IAM.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Deploying Global Guardrails as Code.
  • 4. Weekly Project: Automating Identity Center (SSO) Provisioning.
  • Advanced Topic: FinOps & Cost Tagging Architecture
    Build a reusable Terraform module for cost allocation tagging that enforces tag compliance across all accounts. Create a cost chargeback runbook documenting how billing is attributed to OUs and team owners, plus 3 cost optimization recommendations based on your landing zone design.

In Week 2, we built the house; this week, we install the security system. Governance in a Landing Zone is about "Maximum Freedom within a Defined Perimeter." We will explore how to use Service Control Policies (SCPs) to set absolute limits that even an Administrator cannot bypass, ensuring your "Production" accounts remain immutable and secure.

"Good governance is not about saying 'No'; it is about building a 'Yes' that is safe, scalable, and impossible to break."
WEEK 03: GOVERNANCE & GUARDRAILS
FOUNDATION

Chapter 3: Least Privilege & Guardrail Theory

In a multi-account environment, the Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP) is no longer just about individual users; it is about the accounts themselves. A Guardrail is a high-level governance policy that provides ongoing oversight of your environment. Unlike traditional firewalls, guardrails are preventive or detective measures that stay in effect even as your infrastructure evolves.

Preventive vs. Detective Guardrails

A specialist architect must balance the rigidity of prevention with the agility of detection:

  • G1. Preventive Guardrails: These stop an action before it happens (e.g., an SCP that prevents any user in a "Production" OU from deleting CloudTrail logs).
  • G2. Detective Guardrails: These identify when a resource is "Out of Compliance" and trigger an alert or automated remediation (e.g., a rule that flags any S3 bucket that is made public).

Pre-Class Exercises: Preparing for Governance

To prepare for deploying global policies, initialize your Week 3 workspace:

  • Ex 1. Directory Setup: Create /week-03-governance. Inside, create /policies and /sso-config.
  • Ex 2. Policy JSON Drafting: In your /policies folder, create a file named deny-region-lock.json. Write a draft policy that would deny all actions in every region except your primary home region (e.g., us-east-1 or eu-west-1).
  • Ex 3. Identity Audit: List the current IAM users in your management account and identify which ones should be migrated to AWS IAM Identity Center (SSO).

Architect's Note: Service Control Policies (SCPs) do not grant permissions; they only filter them. Even if a user has "AdministratorAccess," an SCP can still block them. This is the ultimate "Root" of cloud security.

WEEK 03: GOVERNANCE & GUARDRAILS
TEACHING NOTES

The Policy Intersection: SCPs vs. Boundaries vs. IAM

In a Landing Zone, permissions are not just about what a user has; they are about what the environment permits. We use a "defense-in-depth" approach where three distinct policy types intersect to determine the final effective permission.

1. Service Control Policies (SCPs)

SCPs are the "Global Filters." They are applied at the OU or Account level within the Organization. An SCP defines the maximum available permissions for an entire account. Even if a user is given "Full Administrator" rights via IAM, if an SCP denies a service (e.g., "Deny S3"), that user can never access S3.

2. Permission Boundaries

These are the "Safety Nets" for decentralized administration. We use Permission Boundaries to limit the maximum permissions an IAM entity can have. This is essential when you want to allow a developer to create their own IAM roles but want to ensure they cannot create a role with more power than themselves.

3. Identity-Based (IAM) Policies

These are the "Standard Access" grants. They are attached directly to users, groups, or roles. They define exactly what actions the identity can perform on specific resources.

The Evaluation Logic Rule:

Implicit Deny → SCP Check → Boundary Check → IAM Check → Final Decision.
If any layer contains an explicit "Deny," the request is rejected immediately, regardless of what other layers say.

WEEK 03: GOVERNANCE & GUARDRAILS
HANDS-ON LAB

Lab 3.1: Deploying Global Guardrails as Code

This session focuses on codifying a "Region Lock" policy. This is a standard enterprise guardrail used to ensure data residency compliance and to prevent cost spikes from resources deployed in non-standard regions.

Task 1: Define the Region Lock Policy

Create a guardrails.tf file in your /week-03-governance/policies directory. This code defines the policy and attaches it to your "Workloads" OU.

resource "aws_organizations_policy" "region_lock" { name = "RegionLockPolicy" description = "Deny all regions except us-east-1" content = jsonencode({ Version = "2012-10-17" Statement = [{ Sid = "DenyAllOutsideUSEast1" Effect = "Deny" NotAction = ["iam:*", "organizations:*", "route53:*"] Resource = "*" Condition = { StringNotEquals = { "aws:RequestedRegion": ["us-east-1"] } } }] }) } resource "aws_organizations_policy_attachment" "workloads_attach" { policy_id = aws_organizations_policy.region_lock.id target_id = aws_organizations_organizational_unit.workloads.id }

Task 2: Testing and Denial Verification

After running terraform apply, switch to a child account within the Workloads OU and attempt to create a resource in a forbidden region (e.g., us-west-2).

  • [ ] Run terraform apply to push the SCP to the Cloud Organization.
  • [ ] Attempt to create a VPC in us-west-2 and confirm the "Explicit Deny" error.
  • [ ] Document the error message in your ENVIRONMENT_AUDIT.md for this week.

Architectural Defense: Why did we include iam:* and route53:* in the NotAction list? Hint: Think about "Global Services" that do not belong to a specific region.

WEEK 03: GOVERNANCE & GUARDRAILS
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 3: Automating Identity Center (SSO) Provisioning

The objective of this project is to eliminate "Long-Lived Credentials" by deploying a centralized Identity Center. You will codify Permission Sets and Assignments to allow seamless access across your multi-account hierarchy.

Project Deliverables

1. The "Specialist" Permission Set

Create a Terraform resource for an SSO Permission Set titled LandingZoneSpecialist. It must include a session duration of 4 hours and be attached to the ReadOnlyAccess managed policy.

2. Automated Account Assignments

Codify the assignment of your "Specialist" user to both the Security and Workloads OUs. Use Terraform for_each loops to ensure that as new accounts are added to these OUs, permissions are inherited automatically.

3. The Identity Migration Report

Document the transition in IDENTITY_STRATEGY.md. Explain why moving to SSO reduces the risk of credential leakage compared to static IAM Access Keys.

Phase I Completion Checklist

Governance Proof Is your Region-Lock SCP active and tested?
Identity Proof Can you log in via the SSO portal without using an IAM password?
Repository Health Does your GitHub repository show 14+ days of consistent activity?

Career Strategy: Transitioning an organization from IAM Users to Identity Center is one of the most common "Quick Wins" a consultant can provide. This project is your template for that high-value service.

PHASE I ASSESSMENT
FOUNDATIONAL STRATEGY & MULTI-ACCOUNT THEORY

Phase I Knowledge Assessment

📋 Assessment Overview

Questions: 15 multiple-choice questions

Time Estimate: 45-60 minutes

Passing Score: 11/15 (73%)

Coverage: Weeks 1-3 (Account Strategy, Identity, Governance)

1. What is the primary purpose of the "Blast Radius" concept in Landing Zone design?

  • A) To minimize cloud costs by reducing resource usage
  • B) To limit the impact of security breaches or configuration errors
  • C) To optimize network performance across regions
  • D) To standardize resource naming conventions
Answer: B. The Blast Radius ensures that issues in one account don't cascade to affect the entire organization.

2. Which AWS service provides the foundational structure for multi-account governance and centralized billing?

  • A) AWS Control Tower
  • B) AWS Organizations
  • C) AWS Resource Access Manager
  • D) AWS Systems Manager
Answer: B. AWS Organizations is the core service that enables account management, SCPs, and consolidated billing.

3. In AWS Organizations, what is the hierarchical structure used to group accounts with similar purposes?

  • A) Resource Groups
  • B) Organizational Units (OUs)
  • C) Security Groups
  • D) Auto Scaling Groups
Answer: B. OUs allow you to organize accounts hierarchically and apply policies at different levels.

4. What is the primary function of AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly SSO)?

  • A) To manage EC2 instances across accounts
  • B) To provide centralized user authentication and authorization
  • C) To monitor resource usage and costs
  • D) To automate infrastructure deployment
Answer: B. Identity Center enables single sign-on access to multiple AWS accounts with centralized user management.

5. Which policy type in AWS Organizations acts as a permissions boundary that cannot be overridden by IAM policies?

  • A) Identity-based policies
  • B) Resource-based policies
  • C) Service Control Policies (SCPs)
  • D) Trust policies
Answer: C. SCPs define the maximum permissions available to accounts in an OU, acting as guardrails.

6. What is the recommended approach for managing IAM users in a Landing Zone?

  • A) Create individual IAM users in each account
  • B) Use the root account for all administrative tasks
  • C) Centralize identity management and avoid long-term IAM credentials
  • D) Share access keys between team members
Answer: C. Centralized identity management through Identity Center reduces credential management overhead and improves security.

7. Which account type should contain the foundational AWS services and organizational policies?

  • A) Workload accounts
  • B) Management account
  • C) Log Archive account
  • D) Audit account
Answer: B. The Management account is the root of the organization and contains the master policies and billing.

8. What is the purpose of a "Permission Set" in AWS IAM Identity Center?

  • A) To define user passwords
  • B) To specify the level of access users have in assigned accounts
  • C) To manage MFA settings
  • D) To track user login history
Answer: B. Permission Sets define reusable access patterns that can be assigned to users across multiple accounts.

9. Which principle should guide the design of Organizational Units in a Landing Zone?

  • A) Minimize the number of OUs for simplicity
  • B) Group accounts by business function and risk profile
  • C) Create one OU per AWS region
  • D) Base OU structure on team size
Answer: B. OU design should reflect business needs, compliance requirements, and risk management strategies.

10. What is the effect of attaching an SCP with a "Deny" statement to an OU?

  • A) It grants permissions to all accounts in the OU
  • B) It overrides any IAM Allow policies for the denied actions
  • C) It only affects the root user of the Management account
  • D) It requires approval from the account owner to take effect
Answer: B. SCP Deny statements create absolute boundaries that cannot be overridden by IAM policies.

11. Which account should be used for day-to-day administrative tasks in a Landing Zone?

  • A) Workload accounts
  • B) A dedicated administrative workload account
  • C) The Log Archive account
  • D) Any account with administrative permissions
Answer: B. The Management account should be protected and not used for routine operations to minimize security risks.

12. What is the primary benefit of using AWS Organizations for cost management?

  • A) It automatically reduces costs
  • B) It provides consolidated billing and cost allocation tags
  • C) It replaces individual account billing
  • D) It manages resource reservations
Answer: B. Consolidated billing simplifies cost tracking and enables cost allocation across the organization.

13. Which AWS service helps prevent the creation of resources in unauthorized regions?

  • A) AWS Config
  • B) Service Control Policies
  • C) AWS Budgets
  • D) AWS Trusted Advisor
Answer: B. SCPs can restrict actions based on region, providing geographic compliance controls.

14. What should be the first step when designing a Landing Zone OU structure?

  • A) Create as many OUs as possible
  • B) Understand business requirements and compliance needs
  • C) Copy an existing organization's structure
  • D) Focus only on technical requirements
Answer: B. OU design should align with business objectives, security requirements, and operational needs.

15. Which principle is violated by using long-term IAM access keys in a Landing Zone?

  • A) Least Privilege
  • B) Defense in Depth
  • C) Zero Trust
  • D) All of the above
Answer: D. Long-term credentials increase risk and violate modern security principles that emphasize temporary, identity-based access.

🎯 Phase I Completion

Congratulations on completing Phase I! You now understand the foundational principles of multi-account governance.

Proceed to Phase II to learn about network architecture and shared services. Your mentor will review your Phase I work before advancing.

Phase I Assessment
Phase II

Networking &
Shared Services

Moving from identity to connectivity. This phase covers the construction of the "Data Highway" of your Landing Zone—designing Hub-and-Spoke architectures, centralizing security inspections, and automating cross-account logging.

"In the cloud, networking is no longer about cables and switches; it is a software-defined conversation between distributed entities."
Week 04

Enterprise Network Architectures & Transit Control

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Inter-account Communication Theory & Transit Gateway Logic.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: Transit Gateway vs. VPC Peering at Scale.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Automated Network Hub Deployment using Terraform.
  • 4. Weekly Project: Building a Centralized Logging & Audit Repository.

In Phase I, we secured the identity of our accounts. In Phase II, we connect them. This week introduces the "Hub-and-Spoke" model, a critical design pattern for managing complex traffic flows between hundreds of VPCs without the operational overhead of a mesh network. We will master the Transit Gateway—the regional virtual router for your Landing Zone.

"A network is not just a collection of connections; it is the nervous system of your infrastructure. If the path is unclear, the data is lost."
WEEK 04: HUB-AND-SPOKE NETWORKING
FOUNDATION

Chapter 4: Inter-account Communication Theory

In Phase I, we isolated our accounts to reduce the Blast Radius. However, isolation without connectivity is an island, not an ecosystem. Inter-account Communication Theory focuses on how to allow disparate VPCs (Virtual Private Clouds) to talk to each other securely without creating a "Spaghetti Mesh" of manual connections.

The Evolution: From Peering to Hub-and-Spoke

As a Landing Zone Specialist, you must understand why we move away from traditional VPC peering for large-scale deployments:

  • T1. Transit Limitations: Standard VPC peering is non-transitive. If VPC A is peered to VPC B, and VPC B is peered to VPC C, VPC A cannot talk to VPC C through B.
  • T2. The "Hub" Concept: By creating a central "Network Account," we deploy a Transit Gateway that acts as a regional router. Every other account (Spoke) simply "attaches" to this hub.
  • T3. Route Table Management: Instead of managing hundreds of peering routes, we centralize routing logic in the Hub, allowing for global traffic inspection.

Pre-Class Exercises: Initializing the Network Spoke

  • Ex 1. Repository Expansion: Create /week-04-networking. Inside, create /hub-config and /spoke-attachments.
  • Ex 2. CIDR Planning: In a file titled IP_PLAN.md, list three non-overlapping CIDR blocks (e.g., 10.1.0.0/16, 10.2.0.0/16, 10.3.0.0/16) to be used for your Hub and two Spoke accounts.
  • Ex 3. Network Hub Initialization: Prepare a Terraform provider file in /hub-config that targets your dedicated "Network" or "Infrastructure" account.

Architect's Note: Overlapping IP ranges are the death of a Landing Zone. Always use your IP_PLAN as the source of truth before running terraform apply on any VPC.

WEEK 04: HUB-AND-SPOKE NETWORKING
TEACHING NOTES

Transit Gateway vs. VPC Peering at Scale

As a Landing Zone Specialist, you will often be asked: "Why can't we just use Peering?" The answer lies in the complexity of n(n-1)/2. As the number of VPCs grows, the number of peering connections required for a full mesh becomes unmanageable.

1. The Mesh Complexity (VPC Peering)

VPC Peering is a 1-to-1 relationship. If you have 10 VPCs that all need to talk to each other, you need 45 separate peering connections. Each connection requires manual acceptance and individual route table updates. Peering is excellent for high-performance, low-latency links between two specific points, but it does not scale for enterprise "East-West" traffic.

2. The Centralized Router (Transit Gateway)

Transit Gateway (TGW) acts as a regional network hub. You attach your VPCs to the TGW, and it handles the routing between them.

  • • Simplified Routing: One attachment per VPC instead of dozens of peers.
  • • Transitive Routing: VPC A can talk to VPC C through the TGW.
  • • Central Inspection: You can force all traffic through a "Security VPC" for deep packet inspection.

The Architect's Rule of Thumb:

Use VPC Peering for simple, low-latency connections between two accounts within the same region. Use Transit Gateway for any environment involving more than 3-5 VPCs or requiring cross-account centralized security.

WEEK 04: HUB-AND-SPOKE NETWORKING
HANDS-ON LAB

Lab 4.1: Automated Network Hub Deployment

This lab focuses on the creation of the Transit Gateway and the initialization of its Route Tables. This hub will eventually serve as the single point of entry and exit for all inter-VPC traffic.

Task 1: Provision the Transit Gateway

Create a tgw.tf file in your /week-04-networking/hub-config directory. This code defines the router and disables "Default Route Propagation" to allow for manual, secure route control.

resource "aws_ec2_transit_gateway" "hub" { description = "Main Regional Network Hub" default_route_table_association = "disable" default_route_table_propagation = "disable" auto_accept_shared_attachments = "enable" tags = { Name = "hex-tgw-hub-01" } }

Task 2: Resource Access Manager (RAM) Sharing

Because the TGW lives in the Network account, we must "Share" it with the rest of the Organization so that Spoke accounts can see it and attach to it.

  • [ ] Enable Resource Access Manager sharing for your Organization.
  • [ ] Run terraform apply and capture the tgw_id output.
  • [ ] Verify the TGW status is available in the Cloud Console.

Architectural Defense: Why did we disable default_route_table_association? In an enterprise Landing Zone, we never want a new VPC to automatically talk to everything else. We want Explicit Intent for every connection.

WEEK 04: HUB-AND-SPOKE NETWORKING
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 4: Building a Centralized Logging & Audit Repository

The objective of this project is to implement a Single Source of Truth for network forensics. You will configure VPC Flow Logs to capture traffic patterns from your Spokes and stream them into a centralized S3 bucket located in your Security or Audit account.

Project Deliverables

1. The Centralized S3 Log Bucket

Deploy a cross-account S3 bucket with a policy that allows delivery.logs.amazonaws.com to write logs from any account within your Organization. Ensure Object Lock or MFA Delete is enabled for tamper-resistance.

2. Automated VPC Flow Log Activation

Create a Terraform module that activates VPC Flow Logs for every "Spoke" VPC created in Lab 4.1. The destination must point to your centralized S3 bucket ARN.

3. The Traffic Visibility Report

In a file named LOGGING_STRATEGY.md, document how centralized logging simplifies the job of a security auditor. Explain the "Read-Only" access pattern for this account.

Submission Checklist

Cross-Account Trust Does your S3 Bucket Policy include an Organization ID condition?
Data Immutability Is Versioning enabled on your central log bucket?
IaC Consistency Are your Network Hub and Logging configurations in separate Git folders?

Career Strategy: Being able to demonstrate a "Tamper-Proof Audit Trail" is the gold standard for compliance-heavy industries (FinTech, Healthcare). This project proves you can architect for compliance, not just connectivity.

Week 05

Shared Services, Private Link & Secure Connectivity

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Service Consumption vs. Network Connectivity.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: Shared VPC (GCP/AWS) vs. Private Link/Endpoint Services.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Creating a Cross-Account Private Endpoint.
  • 4. Weekly Project: Deploying a "Hidden" Internal Service Provider.

Connectivity is the first step, but security is the ultimate goal. This week, we explore how to share network resources across accounts without full peering. We will master Shared VPCs for centralized IP management and Private Link (VPC Endpoints) to allow services to talk privately, ensuring that sensitive internal data never touches the "Dirty Internet."

"The most secure connection is the one the world cannot see. True architectural mastery is making the complex feel invisible."
WEEK 05: SHARED VPCS & PRIVATE LINK
FOUNDATION

Chapter 5: Service Consumption Theory

In a mature Landing Zone, we distinguish between Network Connectivity (the ability to route packets) and Service Consumption (the ability to access an application). Service Consumption Theory dictates that we should minimize the number of VPCs managed by application teams to reduce "IP Sprawl" and centralize security controls.

Centralizing the Network Fabric

To achieve high-efficiency service delivery, we utilize two primary patterns:

  • S1. The Shared VPC Pattern: A central "Network Account" owns the VPC and its subnets. These subnets are "shared" with application accounts. Developers can deploy resources into these subnets but cannot change the underlying network configuration.
  • S2. The Private Link Pattern: This allows a service provider in one VPC to expose their application to a consumer in another VPC via a private endpoint. Traffic stays on the provider's backbone, bypassing the public internet entirely.

Pre-Class Exercises: Initializing Private Endpoints

  • Ex 1. Repository Expansion: Create /week-05-shared-services. Inside, create /shared-vpc-config and /endpoint-logic.
  • Ex 2. Resource Tagging Policy: Draft a TAGGING_GUIDE.md that defines how shared subnets should be tagged so that Terraform can dynamically discover them in consumer accounts.
  • Ex 3. CLI Discovery: Run a command to list all available VPC Endpoints in your current region and redirect the JSON output to available_services.json in your exercises folder.

Architect's Note: Shared VPCs are excellent for internal trust, but Private Link is the king of "Zero Trust" networking. If you don't need full routing access, always prefer an Endpoint over a Peer.

WEEK 05: SHARED VPCS & PRIVATE LINK
TEACHING NOTES

Shared VPC vs. Private Link/Endpoints

Selecting the right service delivery model is critical for maintaining a clean IP space and high security. While both patterns allow cross-account communication, they solve for different organizational needs.

1. Shared VPC (Administrative Centralization)

In a Shared VPC model, a central Host Account owns the network infrastructure (VPC, Subnets, Route Tables). It shares specific subnets with Service Accounts.

  • • Best For: Organizations that want a single team to manage all IP addresses and network security groups.
  • • Key Benefit: Simplifies network topology by reducing the number of VPCs that need to be peered or attached to a Hub.

2. Private Link / Interface Endpoints (Zero-Trust)

Private Link allows you to expose a service (backed by a Network Load Balancer) to other VPCs as if it were a local IP address in the consumer's subnet.

  • • Best For: Connecting overlapping IP spaces or providing a service to accounts that should not have full network access to the provider.
  • • Key Benefit: One-way connectivity. The consumer can talk to the service, but the service cannot initiate a connection back to the consumer.

Architect's Decision Matrix:

Does the consumer need to see the entire network? Shared VPC.
Does the consumer only need to access a specific API or Database? Private Link.

WEEK 05: SHARED VPCS & PRIVATE LINK
HANDS-ON LAB

Lab 5.1: Creating a Cross-Account Private Endpoint

This lab guides you through the process of "publishing" a service via an Endpoint Service and "consuming" it from a different account. This ensures that sensitive data stays within the provider’s backbone.

Task 1: The Provider Configuration

In your /endpoint-logic folder, define the Endpoint Service. You must link this to a Network Load Balancer (NLB) that sits in front of your internal application.

resource "aws_vpc_endpoint_service" "app_provider" { acceptance_required = true network_load_balancer_arns = [aws_lb.internal_nlb.arn] tags = { Name = "hex-private-link-service" } }

Task 2: The Consumer Attachment

Now, switch to your "Workloads" account. You will create the Interface Endpoint that points to the Service Name generated in Task 1.

  • [ ] Provision the aws_vpc_endpoint resource using the Provider's Service Name.
  • [ ] Manually accept the endpoint connection request in the Provider account.
  • [ ] Run a dig or nslookup on the endpoint DNS name to verify it resolves to a private IP.

Architectural Defense: What is the primary security advantage of using an Interface Endpoint over a VPC Peer for an RDS Database? Consider the "Directionality" of the connection.

WEEK 05: SHARED VPCS & PRIVATE LINK
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 5: Deploying a "Hidden" Internal Service Provider

The objective of this project is to build a highly secure, producer-consumer architecture. You will deploy a "Service Provider" in a restricted VPC and allow a "Consumer" in a separate account to access it via Private Link, ensuring the service remains invisible to the outside world.

Project Deliverables

1. The Producer Service Stack

Deploy a simple internal application (like an Nginx server or a private API) behind a Network Load Balancer (NLB) in your Infrastructure account. This application must have no Internet Gateway (IGW) attached.

2. The Private Link "Handshake" Code

Codify the Endpoint Service in Terraform. Include a whitelist of Account IDs that are allowed to discover and connect to your service. Automate the connection acceptance logic where possible.

3. The "Zero-Internet" Proof

In a file named INTERNAL_SERVICE.md, provide evidence that the consumer can reach the service, but that neither the producer nor the consumer uses a public IP address for the transaction.

Submission Checklist

Traffic Path Is all traffic confirmed to stay on the internal provider backbone?
DNS Resolution Does the consumer use a Private DNS name to resolve the endpoint?
IaC Modularity Is the Endpoint Service logic separated into a reusable module?

Career Strategy: Building "Internal Marketplaces" is a key DevOps trend. Demonstrating that you can offer services across accounts securely—without the complexity of massive peering arrangements—is a high-level architectural skill.

Week 06

Automated Security & Detection Engineering

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Continuous Monitoring & Threat Intelligence.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: Delegated Administration for GuardDuty & Security Hub.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Automating Multi-Account Security Enablement.
  • 4. Weekly Project: Building an Incident Response Dashboard.

A Landing Zone is only as strong as its visibility. This week, we move from preventative guardrails to active threat detection. We will implement GuardDuty for intelligent threat monitoring and Security Hub as our "Single Pane of Glass" for compliance. By delegating administration to a dedicated Security Account, we ensure that security teams have the oversight they need without interfering with application workloads.

"Security is not a point-in-time achievement; it is a continuous state of awareness. Visibility is the first step toward resilience."
WEEK 06: CENTRALIZED SECURITY
FOUNDATION

Chapter 6: Continuous Monitoring Theory

In a traditional data center, security was often a "point-in-time" exercise—an annual audit or a quarterly scan. In a Landing Zone, the speed of change makes periodic checks obsolete. Continuous Monitoring Theory posits that security must be an automated, always-on function that identifies threats and configuration drift the moment they occur.

The Pillars of Automated Defense

To achieve a resilient security posture, we implement two distinct but complementary layers:

  • M1. Threat Intelligence (Active Defense): Using machine learning to analyze VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, and DNS logs to identify malicious patterns, such as crypto-mining, credential exfiltration, or port scanning.
  • M2. Compliance Monitoring (Posture Management): Aggregating security findings against industry standards (like CIS Benchmarks or PCI-DSS) to provide a "Security Score" for the entire organization.

Pre-Class Exercises: Initializing Security Delegation

  • Ex 1. Repository Expansion: Create /week-06-security. Inside, create /detection-config and /compliance-rules.
  • Ex 2. Delegated Admin Identification: Retrieve the Account ID of your "Security" or "Audit" account. You will need this to delegate administrative control from the Management account.
  • Ex 3. Baseline Audit: Run a manual check on your "Workloads" account to see if any S3 buckets are public or if the "Root" user has an active access key. Document these as "Pre-Automation" findings.

Architect's Note: Centralization is the key to sanity. Never log into ten different accounts to check security status; always delegate administration to a single "Security" account to act as your Command Center.

WEEK 06: CENTRALIZED SECURITY
TEACHING NOTES

Delegated Administration: GuardDuty & Security Hub

Delegated Administration is the mechanism that allows a "Member Account" to act as the administrator for a specific service across the entire Organization. This follows the Principle of Least Privilege by ensuring the Management Account is used strictly for billing and structural changes, not daily security operations.

1. Amazon GuardDuty (Threat Detection)

When GuardDuty is delegated to the Security Account, that account gains the ability to:

  • • Auto-Enable: Automatically enable GuardDuty for any new account that joins the Organization.
  • • Aggregate Findings: View all threats (e.g., unusual API calls, known malicious IP interactions) in one central dashboard.

2. AWS Security Hub (Posture Management)

Security Hub acts as the "Aggregator of Aggregators." It ingests findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, and IAM Access Analyzer.

  • • Compliance Scoring: View a consolidated security score based on CIS Foundations or AWS Foundational Security Best Practices.
  • • Central Remediation: Trigger automated actions (like disabling an IAM key) from the delegated Security Account.

The Architect's Goal:

By the end of this week, you should never have to log into an application account to see if it is secure. If a threat exists in "Spoke-A," you should see it, analyze it, and mitigate it from "Security-Hub."

WEEK 06: CENTRALIZED SECURITY
HANDS-ON LAB

Lab 6.1: Automating Multi-Account Security

This lab demonstrates the "One-Click" enablement of security services across an entire Organization. This automation ensures that no account—old or new—ever operates without active threat detection.

Task 1: The Delegation Handshake

In your /detection-config folder, use the Management Account provider to delegate GuardDuty to your Security Account ID.

resource "aws_guardduty_organization_admin_account" "security_delegate" { admin_account_id = var.security_account_id } # Ensure Security Hub is also delegated resource "aws_securityhub_organization_admin_account" "security_hub_delegate" { admin_account_id = var.security_account_id }

Task 2: Automatic Enrollment Logic

Now, using the Security Account provider, configure the organization to automatically enable GuardDuty for all new members.

  • [ ] Set auto_enable = true in the aws_guardduty_organization_configuration resource.
  • [ ] Run terraform apply and verify that the "Workloads" account is now listed as a "Member" in the GuardDuty console of the Security Account.
  • [ ] Repeat this logic for Security Hub to establish your centralized compliance dashboard.

Architectural Defense: Why do we perform the delegation in the Management account but the configuration in the Security account? Think about the "Trust Relationship" between the Organization root and its members.

WEEK 06: CENTRALIZED SECURITY
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 6: Building an Incident Response Dashboard

The objective of this project is to create a Unified Security Overview. You will consolidate findings from GuardDuty and Security Hub into a central reporting structure, enabling rapid identification of high-severity risks across your entire Landing Zone.

Project Deliverables

1. The Centralized Findings Aggregator

Configure Security Hub to ingest findings from at least three integrated services (e.g., GuardDuty, IAM Access Analyzer, and AWS Config). Ensure cross-region aggregation is enabled if your Landing Zone spans multiple regions.

2. High-Severity Alerting Logic

Create a Terraform resource that triggers a notification (e.g., via SNS or a Lambda function) whenever a "Critical" or "High" severity finding is generated in any member account. This ensures immediate awareness of potential breaches.

3. The Security Posture Analysis

In a file named SECURITY_POSTURE.md, analyze the findings generated during the Week 6 labs. Identify the top three risks in your environment and document the automated steps taken to remediate them.

Phase II Progress Checklist

Detection Coverage Is GuardDuty active in every account under your Organization?
Visibility Level Can you see "Workload" findings from the "Security" account dashboard?
Alerting Accuracy Does your notification system distinguish between "Info" and "Critical" events?

Career Strategy: Employers look for architects who can translate "Raw Data" into "Actionable Intelligence." Building this dashboard proves you can handle the operational reality of securing a multi-account enterprise.

Week 07

Observability, Logging & Audit Pipeline

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Forensic Integrity & Log Aggregation Theory.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: The Log Archive Account vs. Monitoring Accounts.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Automating CloudTrail & Flow Log Centralization.
  • 4. Weekly Project: Implementing S3 Object Lock & Tamper-Proofing.

In a multi-account environment, logs are the ultimate source of truth. This week, we architect the "Log Archive" account—a dedicated repository designed to ingest data from every corner of the Landing Zone. We focus on immutability, ensuring that even a compromised administrator cannot delete the digital footprints of their actions. This completes our Phase II networking and shared services foundation.

"Architecture is about what you build; Governance is about what you can prove. A log that cannot be altered is a truth that cannot be denied."
WEEK 07: CENTRALIZED LOGGING
FOUNDATION

Chapter 7: Forensic Integrity Theory

In an enterprise Landing Zone, logs are not just for troubleshooting; they are evidence. Forensic Integrity Theory establishes that for logs to be valid during an incident response or a legal audit, they must be centralized, immutable, and protected by a "Chain of Custody" that prevents even the most privileged users from altering the record.

The Three Pillars of Centralized Logging

To build a "Black Box" for your cloud environment, we adhere to these core principles:

  • L1. Account Isolation: Logs must be shipped out of the account where they were generated and into a dedicated Log Archive account. This prevents an attacker who compromises a workload from deleting their tracks.
  • L2. Write-Once-Read-Many (WORM): Utilizing features like S3 Object Lock or immutable storage policies to ensure that once a log is written, it cannot be deleted or overwritten for a defined retention period.
  • L3. Encryption with Separated Keys: Encrypting logs using KMS keys that are managed in a separate account from the logs themselves, creating a dual-authorization requirement for decryption.

Pre-Class Exercises: Preparing the Archive

  • Ex 1. Repository Expansion: Create /week-07-logging. Inside, create /archive-setup and /trail-config.
  • Ex 2. S3 Policy Drafting: Draft a policy in bucket_policy.json that allows cloudtrail.amazonaws.com from your entire organization to put objects into a specific bucket ARN.
  • Ex 3. Retention Calculator: In RETENTION_PLAN.md, list the legal log retention requirements for your specific industry (e.g., 7 years for Finance, 1 year for General GDPR).

Architect's Note: The Log Archive account is the only account in your Landing Zone where even the Administrator should have "Deny" on s3:DeleteObject. This is the ultimate proof of integrity.

WEEK 07: CENTRALIZED LOGGING
TEACHING NOTES

Log Archive vs. Monitoring Accounts

A common mistake in cloud architecture is treating logging and monitoring as the same task. While they use the same data, their Retention, Access, and Intent are fundamentally different.

1. The Log Archive Account (The Vault)

The Log Archive account is your Immutable Repository. Its primary purpose is long-term retention and audit compliance.

  • • Access: Extremely restricted. Typically, only the Audit/Compliance team has read access, and no one has delete access.
  • • Retention: Defined by legal requirements (e.g., 7 years).
  • • Storage: Optimized for cost using S3 Intelligent-Tiering or Glacier for older logs.

2. The Monitoring/Security Account (The Dashboard)

The Monitoring account (often where Security Hub lives) is your Real-Time Analysis center. It ingests data to trigger alerts and automated remediations.

  • • Access: Security Engineers and DevOps teams have access to view dashboards and logs for troubleshooting.
  • • Retention: Short-term (e.g., 30–90 days) for operational analysis.
  • • Storage: Higher performance (e.g., CloudWatch Logs, OpenSearch) to allow for fast querying and visualization.

Key Separation Rule:

If you delete an alert in the Monitoring account, the underlying log in the Log Archive account must remain untouched. Monitoring is for *now*; Log Archive is for *ever*.

WEEK 07: CENTRALIZED LOGGING
HANDS-ON LAB

Lab 7.1: Automating Log Centralization

This lab focuses on establishing a "one-way" flow of audit data. By the end of this session, your Management account will mandate logging for all child accounts, while the actual data resides in the protected Log Archive silo.

Task 1: Deploying the Organization Trail

Create a central_trail.tf file in your /week-07-logging/trail-config directory. This resource must be deployed using the Management Account provider.

resource "aws_cloudtrail" "org_trail" { name = "hex-org-audit-trail" s3_bucket_name = var.log_archive_bucket_id is_organization_trail = true is_multi_region_trail = true include_global_service_events = true enable_log_file_validation = true tags = { Environment = "Governance" } }

Task 2: Spoke Account Flow Log Connection

Now, update your Workloads account networking code to stream VPC Flow Logs directly to the Log Archive S3 bucket.

  • [ ] Provision aws_flow_log resources for all Spoke VPCs.
  • [ ] Run terraform apply and confirm that log files are appearing in the Log Archive S3 bucket under the correct account prefixes.
  • [ ] Verify Log File Validation is enabled to ensure the digest files can detect any log tampering.

Architectural Defense: Why do we use is_organization_trail = true instead of creating a trail in each account? Hint: Consider what happens if an attacker gains Administrator access in a member account and tries to disable the trail.

WEEK 07: CENTRALIZED LOGGING
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 7: Implementing S3 Object Lock & Tamper-Proofing

The objective of this project is to finalize the Immutable Audit Vault. You will configure your central log bucket to use "Compliance Mode" object locking, effectively placing the logs beyond the reach of any user, including the root account, for a specified duration.

Project Deliverables

1. The WORM Bucket Configuration

Update your /archive-setup Terraform code to enable object_lock_enabled = true. Define a default retention period of 365 days in Compliance Mode. Note: This cannot be undone once applied to an object.

2. KMS Key Separation

Create a Customer Managed Key (CMK) in the Security account to encrypt logs stored in the Log Archive account. Document the cross-account key policy that allows the Archive bucket to use this key.

3. The Compliance Validation Report

In a file named COMPLIANCE_AUDIT.md, attempt to delete a log file using the CLI and capture the "Access Denied" error. Explain why this protection is mandatory for financial or medical data residency.

Phase II Final Checklist

Networking Hub Is the Transit Gateway routing traffic correctly between Spokes?
Security Oversight Are GuardDuty findings visible in the Security Hub dashboard?
Forensic Integrity Does the Log Archive account contain immutable CloudTrail logs?

Architect's Note: You have now completed the "Hardening" phase. Your Landing Zone is not just connected; it is monitored, audited, and protected against internal and external threats.

PHASE II ASSESSMENT
NETWORK ARCHITECTURE & SHARED SERVICES

Phase II Knowledge Assessment

📋 Assessment Overview

Questions: 18 multiple-choice questions

Time Estimate: 50-70 minutes

Passing Score: 13/18 (72%)

Coverage: Weeks 4-7 (Transit Gateway, Shared Services, Security, Logging)

1. What is the primary purpose of a Transit Gateway in a Landing Zone?

  • A) To provide internet access to all VPCs
  • B) To enable secure communication between VPCs across accounts
  • C) To manage DNS resolution for the organization
  • D) To store centralized logs from all accounts
Answer: B. Transit Gateway acts as a network hub, enabling secure cross-account VPC communication without complex peering relationships.

2. In a Hub-and-Spoke network architecture, what role does the "Hub" play?

  • A) It contains application workloads
  • B) It provides centralized network services and routing
  • C) It stores user data and databases
  • D) It manages identity and access control
Answer: B. The Hub contains shared network infrastructure like Transit Gateway, VPN endpoints, and centralized egress.

3. Which AWS service enables sharing of Transit Gateway across multiple accounts?

  • A) AWS Organizations
  • B) AWS Resource Access Manager (RAM)
  • C) AWS Systems Manager
  • D) AWS Config
Answer: B. RAM allows sharing Transit Gateway resources across accounts in the same organization.

4. What is the primary benefit of using PrivateLink for shared services?

  • A) It reduces network latency
  • B) It keeps traffic within the AWS network, avoiding internet exposure
  • C) It automatically scales based on demand
  • D) It provides built-in encryption for all traffic
Answer: B. PrivateLink enables secure access to shared services without exposing endpoints to the public internet.

5. Which service is best suited for detecting anomalous API activity across your Landing Zone?

  • A) AWS Config
  • B) AWS GuardDuty
  • C) AWS Inspector
  • D) AWS Trusted Advisor
Answer: B. GuardDuty uses machine learning to identify unusual patterns that may indicate security threats.

6. What is the purpose of a "Log Archive" account in a Landing Zone?

  • A) To run application workloads
  • B) To provide centralized, immutable log storage
  • C) To manage user identities
  • D) To host shared network services
Answer: B. The Log Archive account ensures logs cannot be tampered with, even by privileged users.

7. Which AWS service provides organization-wide visibility into security findings?

  • A) AWS CloudTrail
  • B) AWS Security Hub
  • C) AWS Config
  • D) AWS Systems Manager
Answer: B. Security Hub aggregates and prioritizes security findings from multiple AWS services.

8. What is CloudTrail primarily used for in a Landing Zone?

  • A) Cost monitoring
  • B) API activity auditing
  • C) Resource configuration management
  • D) Network traffic analysis
Answer: B. CloudTrail records all API calls, providing an audit trail of who did what and when.

9. Which account type should contain the Security Hub master configuration?

  • A) Management account
  • B) Audit account
  • C) Log Archive account
  • D) Workload accounts
Answer: B. The Audit account serves as the Security Hub administrator for centralized security monitoring.

10. What is the benefit of enabling CloudTrail organization trails?

  • A) It reduces logging costs
  • B) It automatically enables logging for all current and future accounts
  • C) It provides real-time alerting
  • D) It encrypts log data automatically
Answer: B. Organization trails ensure comprehensive API logging across all accounts without manual configuration.

11. Which service should you use to share a Transit Gateway with accounts outside your organization?

  • A) AWS Resource Access Manager
  • B) AWS Organizations
  • C) VPC Peering
  • D) Direct Connect
Answer: A. RAM can share resources with external accounts, though this is less common in Landing Zones.

12. What is the primary security benefit of using GuardDuty in a multi-account setup?

  • A) It provides automated remediation
  • B) It correlates findings across accounts for broader threat detection
  • C) It replaces the need for Security Groups
  • D) It manages IAM permissions
Answer: B. GuardDuty's cross-account correlation helps identify organization-wide attack patterns.

13. Which account should have read-only access to the Log Archive S3 buckets?

  • A) Management account
  • B) Audit account
  • C) All workload accounts
  • D) No accounts should have access
Answer: B. The Audit account needs read access for compliance monitoring and investigations.

14. What is the role of Transit Gateway Route Tables in network segmentation?

  • A) They encrypt network traffic
  • B) They control which VPCs can communicate with each other
  • C) They manage IP address allocation
  • D) They provide internet connectivity
Answer: B. Route tables enable isolation between different network segments while allowing controlled communication.

15. Which service provides automated compliance monitoring and remediation in a Landing Zone?

  • A) AWS Config
  • B) AWS CloudTrail
  • C) AWS GuardDuty
  • D) AWS Security Hub
Answer: A. AWS Config continuously monitors resource configurations and can trigger automated remediation.

16. What is the primary purpose of VPC Flow Logs in a Network Hub?

  • A) To monitor application performance
  • B) To provide network traffic visibility for security analysis
  • C) To manage IP address assignments
  • D) To optimize routing decisions
Answer: B. Flow logs capture network traffic metadata for security monitoring and troubleshooting.

17. Which account architecture ensures that security logs cannot be deleted by any user, including administrators?

  • A) Management account with SCPs
  • B) Separate Log Archive account with strict IAM policies
  • C) Workload accounts with GuardDuty
  • D) Audit account with Security Hub
Answer: B. The Log Archive account isolates logs from operational accounts, preventing tampering.

18. What is the benefit of using organization trails over individual account trails?

  • A) They cost less per account
  • B) They provide a single source of truth for all organizational activity
  • C) They automatically encrypt all log data
  • D) They enable real-time alerting
Answer: B. Organization trails consolidate all API activity into a single, comprehensive audit trail.

🎯 Phase II Completion

Congratulations on completing Phase II! Your Landing Zone now has secure network connectivity and comprehensive monitoring.

Proceed to Phase III to master automation and platform governance. Your mentor will review your Phase II infrastructure before advancing.

Phase II Assessment
Phase III

Automation &
Customizations

Moving from manual configuration to programmatic scaling. This phase covers the creation of the "Account Factory"—automating the provisioning, hardening, and baseline customization of new accounts to ensure every "vended" environment is secure and compliant by default.

"True scale is achieved when the manual becomes automated, and the automated becomes invisible. We are no longer building accounts; we are building the machine that builds accounts."
Week 08

Automated Account Factory & Self-Service Provisioning

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Account Vending Theory & Scalable Provisioning.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: Terraform Organizations vs. Control Tower Account Factory.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Creating an Account Vending Machine (AVM) with Terraform.
  • 4. Weekly Project: Automating Baseline Enrollment for New Spokes.

As a Landing Zone Specialist, you must move beyond manual account creation. This week, we architect the "Account Factory"—a standardized process for "vending" new accounts through code. We will implement the logic that ensures every new environment is automatically placed in the correct Organizational Unit, tagged appropriately, and connected to our core networking and security fabrics the moment it is born.

"Scalability is the ability to repeat success without repeating effort. The Account Factory is where infrastructure becomes a product."
WEEK 08: THE ACCOUNT FACTORY
FOUNDATION

Chapter 8: Account Vending Theory

As an organization grows, the manual creation of accounts becomes a bottleneck and a security risk. Account Vending Theory treats the account as a "Product." Instead of a one-off task, we create a standardized delivery pipeline that ensures every account—whether for a Sandbox, Development, or Production environment—starts with the same baseline security, identity, and networking configurations.

The Mechanics of "Vending"

A specialist architect views account vending as a four-stage orchestration:

  • V1. Request & Approval: A developer or project lead requests an account with specific parameters (Owner, Cost Center, Environment Type).
  • V2. Core Provisioning: The infrastructure-as-code (IaC) layer triggers the creation of the account entity and places it into the correct Organizational Unit (OU).
  • V3. Baseline Hardening: Governance policies (SCPs), security services (GuardDuty), and logging (CloudTrail) are automatically enabled and locked.
  • V4. Network Integration: The account is automatically attached to the Transit Gateway hub or joined to a Shared VPC, making it "Day 1" ready for workloads.

Pre-Class Exercises: Drafting the Factory

  • Ex 1. Repository Setup: Create /week-08-account-factory. Inside, create /modules/account-vending-machine and /accounts-inventory.
  • Ex 2. Variable Definition: Create a variables.tf that defines the required metadata for any new account (e.g., email, account_name, environment_tag).
  • Ex 3. Governance Check: Identify which Service Control Policies (SCPs) from Week 3 must be automatically attached to any new account vended by the factory.

Architect's Note: The goal is not just "Speed," but "Consistency." A factory-vended account is safe because it is impossible for a human to forget a security step in a coded pipeline.

WEEK 08: THE ACCOUNT FACTORY
TEACHING NOTES

Terraform Organizations vs. Control Tower

Deciding between a custom-coded factory and a managed service is a pivotal moment for any architect. Both paths achieve automation, but they differ significantly in their long-term maintenance and customizability.

1. AWS Control Tower (Managed Vending)

Control Tower provides an out-of-the-box Account Factory. It is ideal for organizations that want the cloud provider to manage the underlying orchestration of OUs, accounts, and mandatory guardrails.
