  • • Pros: Simplified setup; managed guardrails; integrated dashboard for compliance status.
  • • Cons: Limited customization; can be difficult to "undo" certain managed configurations; potentially higher complexity when integrating with 3rd party tools.

2. Terraform-Driven AVM (Custom Vending)

A custom Account Vending Machine (AVM) built in Terraform gives you complete control over the provisioning logic.

  • • Pros: Complete flexibility; multi-cloud compatibility (conceptually); fits perfectly into existing CI/CD pipelines.
  • • Cons: High maintenance; your team is responsible for the integrity of the provisioning code and guardrail enforcement.

The Decision Metric:

If your client requires Standard Compliance with minimal overhead, choose Control Tower. If your client requires Deep Customization and uses complex Infrastructure-as-Code patterns, build a Terraform AVM.

WEEK 08: THE ACCOUNT FACTORY
HANDS-ON LAB

Lab 8.1: Creating a Custom Terraform AVM

This lab guides you through building a reusable Terraform module that handles the lifecycle of a cloud account. This module is the cornerstone for Hexadigitall’s automation strategy, ensuring every environment is a "Clone of the Best."

Task 1: The Account Provisioning Module

Navigate to /week-08-account-factory/modules/account-vending-machine and create a main.tf. This code creates the account and places it in the designated OU.

resource "aws_organizations_account" "vended_account" { name = var.account_name email = var.account_email parent_id = var.target_ou_id # This allows the Management account to manage the new account role_name = "OrganizationAccountAccessRole" tags = { ManagedBy = "Hexadigitall-AVM" Environment = var.env_tag } }

Task 2: Testing the Vending Logic

In your /accounts-inventory folder, call the module to create a new "Sandbox" account. Use a unique email address (many providers allow email+suffix@domain.com).

  • [ ] Run terraform plan and confirm that one account resource is being created.
  • [ ] Run terraform apply and monitor the Organizations console.
  • [ ] Once the account is active, log in via SSO to verify the OrganizationAccountAccessRole is present.

Architectural Defense: What happens if you run terraform destroy on an aws_organizations_account? Crucial Note: In AWS, deleting the resource from Terraform only removes it from the state/organization; it does not "delete" the account itself. This requires manual closure or specific API calls.

WEEK 08: THE ACCOUNT FACTORY
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 8: Automating Baseline Enrollment

The objective of this project is to create an Autonomic Landing Zone. You will extend your AVM module to perform "Post-Provisioning logic." This means the code that creates the account will now also reach into that account to set up its initial defenses.

Project Deliverables

1. The "Post-Provision" Handshake

Update the AVM module to include a second Terraform provider that assumes the OrganizationAccountAccessRole in the newly created account. Use this provider to enable a specific "Baseline" resource (e.g., an S3 Public Access Block at the account level).

2. Governance Alignment

Codify the attachment of the Deny-Leaving-Organization SCP to the vended account. Ensure the AVM module requires a cost_center tag before it will execute, enforcing financial accountability from Day 1.

3. The Provisioning Log

Create a VENDING_REPORT.md that tracks the execution time of your factory. Document the sequence of events from "Resource Created" to "Security Baseline Applied."

Success Criteria

Zero-Touch Config Can you create a fully hardened account with a single 'terraform apply'?
Tagging Compliance Does the new account automatically inherit mandatory tags?
State Integrity Is the vended account's metadata correctly stored in the Management state?

Architect's Insight: In Hexadigitall's world, an account that isn't baselined isn't "live." By forcing baseline enrollment into the AVM, you eliminate the "Security Gap" that occurs between account creation and manual hardening.

Week 09

Account Customizations, Post-Deployment Controls & Drift Guardrails

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Idempotent Customization & Lifecycle Hooks.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: Cloud-Init vs. Terraform Provider Orchestration.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Deploying Regional Baselines & IAM Custom Roles.
  • 4. Weekly Project: The "Environment-Aware" Customizer.

Creation is only half the battle. A production account requires different "DNA" than a sandbox environment. This week, we master the art of Post-Deployment Logic—using Terraform hooks and provider aliasing to inject environment-specific configurations into vended accounts. We move beyond universal baselines to create tailored infrastructure that respects the unique requirements of the workloads it will host.

"Standardization provides the floor, but customization provides the ceiling. Architectural excellence is found in the balance between the two."
WEEK 09: ACCOUNT CUSTOMIZATIONS
FOUNDATION

Chapter 9: Idempotent Customization Theory

In a mature Landing Zone, customization is not a one-time script; it is a continuous state. Idempotent Customization Theory dictates that our post-deployment logic must be able to run a thousand times and result in the same configuration every time, without side effects or errors.

The Customization Lifecycle

To scale Hexadigitall Tech deployments, we categorize customizations into three distinct tiers:

  • T1. Global Baselines: Mandatory settings for every account (e.g., IAM password policies, disabling unused regions).
  • T2. OU-Specific Logic: Configurations unique to a business unit (e.g., specialized VPC endpoints for the Data Science team).
  • T3. Environment Overrides: Tuning resources based on risk (e.g., multi-region failover for 'Prod', single-zone for 'Dev').

Pre-Class Exercises: Mapping the Logic

  • Ex 1. Repository Extension: Create /week-09-customizations. Inside, create /modules/baseline-roles and /logic-gateways.
  • Ex 2. Drafting the "DNA": In DNA_MATRIX.md, list three resources that should exist in a "Production" account but should be absent in a "Sandbox" account to save costs.
  • Ex 3. Alias Practice: Research the Terraform alias keyword. Write a snippet that defines two different providers for the same AWS account using different IAM roles.

Architect's Note: Never use a script to "push" configuration. Use Terraform to "declare" the state. If a human manually changes a setting, the next Terraform run should quietly and firmly change it back.

WEEK 09: ACCOUNT CUSTOMIZATIONS
TEACHING NOTES

Cloud-Init vs. Terraform Orchestration

To build a cohesive Landing Zone, we must distinguish between Infrastructure Bootstrapping (the account) and OS Bootstrapping (the instance).

1. Cloud-Init (The "Inside-Out" Approach)

Cloud-init is a multi-distribution package that handles early initialization of a cloud instance.

  • • Scope: Local to the OS (users, packages, files).
  • • Use Case: Joining a node to a Kubernetes cluster or installing Nginx on first boot.

2. Terraform Orchestration (The "Outside-In" Approach)

This involves using Terraform providers to reach into a newly created account and configure the API-level resources.

  • • Scope: Account-wide (IAM Roles, S3 buckets, VPCs).
  • • Use Case: Deploying the Hexadigitall Tech security baseline or cross-account peering.

The Golden Rule of Customization:

Use Terraform to build the Container (the account and network). Use Cloud-Init (or Ansible/Puppet) to configure the Contents (the server software).

WEEK 09: ACCOUNT CUSTOMIZATIONS
HANDS-ON LAB

Lab 9.1: Regional Baselines & Custom IAM

A Landing Zone architect must ensure that no region is left unmonitored. This lab utilizes Terraform for_each loops to deploy resources across multiple regions simultaneously and establishes a cross-account trust for auditing.

Task 1: The Multi-Region EBS Baseline

Security best practice requires that all EBS volumes be encrypted at rest. We will enforce this account-wide across all active regions.

# Example of enforcing EBS encryption by default resource "aws_ebs_encryption_by_default" "enforced" { enabled = true }

Task 2: Deploying the 'HexaAuditor' Role

This task establishes a role that allows the Security account to assume a read-only position in the new Spoke account for compliance checks.

  • [ ] Define a Trust Policy that allows arn:aws:iam::[Security_Account_ID]:root to assume the role.
  • [ ] Attach the ReadOnlyAccess AWS managed policy to the new HexaAuditor role.
  • [ ] Use a Terraform provider with an alias to deploy this resource specifically into your "Sandbox" account.

Architectural Defense: Why do we prefer creating a custom HexaAuditor role instead of just using the default OrganizationAccountAccessRole for security audits? (Hint: Think about the Scope of Permissions).

WEEK 09: ACCOUNT CUSTOMIZATIONS
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 9: The "Environment-Aware" Customizer

The objective of this project is to implement Conditional Logic within your Terraform AVM. You will build a system that detects the account type (e.g., 'Sandbox' vs. 'Production') and applies a customized set of resources and restrictions based on that context.

Project Deliverables

1. Conditional Baseline Logic

Update your AVM module to use Terraform count or for_each with conditional logic. For example: Enable S3 Versioning and MFA Delete in 'Production' accounts, but keep them optional for 'Dev' to reduce complexity.

2. Regional Lockdown Customization

Implement a "Region Whitelist" that varies by environment. 'Sandbox' accounts should be restricted to a single region (to prevent unexpected costs), while 'Production' should have a multi-region baseline enabled by default.

3. The Customization Matrix

Create a CUSTOMIZATION_LOG.json file that maps account IDs to their applied "DNA" profiles. This file serves as your proof that the AVM successfully differentiated between account types during deployment.

Success Criteria

Profile Differentiation Do 'Dev' and 'Prod' accounts show distinct configurations after a single run?
Resource Idempotency Does rerunning the plan result in "No changes" for both account types?
Logic Scalability Can you add a third account type (e.g., 'Staging') by changing just one variable?

Hexadigitall Strategy: In professional consulting, we don't build one-off environments. We build templates of intent. By making your AVM "environment-aware," you ensure the business logic is baked into the code, not the manual checklist.

Week 10

Platform Delivery with AFT & Terraform Automation

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: GitOps for Account Provisioning.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: AFT Architecture & Pipeline Orchestration.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Configuring the AFT Request Pipeline.
  • 4. Weekly Project: Deploying Global Customizations via AFT.
  • Enterprise Extension: Change Governance & Approval Workflows
    Design change control procedures for account factory updates (separate from deployment mechanics). Document approval decision trees, escalation paths, and rollback authorization criteria. Create a change log template and playbook for communicating infrastructure changes to stakeholders.

Manual Terraform runs and custom AVMs take you far, but for high-velocity enterprises, we need Account Factory for Terraform (AFT). This week, we dismantle the "black box" of AFT to understand how it uses GitOps principles to trigger account creation. We will explore how a single git commit can trigger a series of Step Functions and Lambda workers to provision, harden, and customize an account in a fully decoupled, resilient pipeline.

"Complexity is the enemy of scale. AFT turns the complex orchestration of account management into a simple, predictable stream of code."
WEEK 10: DEEP DIVE: AFT
FOUNDATION

Chapter 10: GitOps for Account Provisioning

As Hexadigitall Technologies moves toward enterprise-grade scaling, we must eliminate the "human-in-the-loop" for infrastructure changes. GitOps for Account Provisioning is a paradigm where the intended state of the entire multi-account environment is defined in Git. When a change is merged, the Account Factory for Terraform (AFT) automatically reconciles the physical cloud state with the code.

The AFT Operational Model

AFT introduces a decoupled architecture that separates the request from the execution:

  • G1. The Request Repository: A simple list of accounts (JSON/HCL). A pull request to this repo is the only way to "vending" a new account.
  • G2. The Global Customization Pipeline: Automated logic that applies the "Hexa-Baseline" (Security Hub, GuardDuty, IAM Roles) to every account AFT manages.
  • G3. The Account Customization Pipeline: Environment-specific logic (e.g., adding a specific VPC for 'Payment-Processing' accounts) triggered automatically after the baseline is set.

Pre-Class Exercises: Architecting the Flow

  • Ex 1. Repository Planning: Create /week-10-aft. Inside, create a folder structure mimicking the four AFT repositories: aft-management, aft-account-requests, aft-global-customizations, and aft-account-customizations.
  • Ex 2. Service Limit Audit: Use the CLI to check the current account limit for your Organization. AFT will fail if the Organization hits its account quota during vending.
  • Ex 3. Webhook Strategy: Draft a WEBHOOK_LOGIC.md explaining how a merge to the 'main' branch in GitHub triggers a CodePipeline in the AFT Management account.

Architect's Note: In an AFT-managed environment, your "Terraform Plan" happens in a Pull Request, and your "Apply" happens in a Merge. This is the highest form of auditability for a Landing Zone Specialist.

WEEK 10: DEEP DIVE: AFT
TEACHING NOTES

AFT Architecture & Orchestration

AFT is not a single tool; it is a Framework of Frameworks. It manages the lifecycle of accounts by coordinating between the Management account (where Organizations lives) and a dedicated AFT Management account (where the automation runs).

1. The Four-Repo Strategy

To maintain separation of concerns, AFT requires four distinct Git repositories:

  • • Account Requests: The triggers for new account creation.
  • • Global Customizations: Resources every account must have.
  • • Account Customizations: Code applied only to specific accounts or OUs.
  • • AFT Management: The code that deploys the AFT engine itself.

2. The Event-Driven Heartbeat

When a request is merged, AFT doesn't just run terraform apply. It triggers a Step Function that:

  • • Verifies the account doesn't already exist.
  • • Calls Control Tower to vend the account.
  • • Waits for the account to be "Ready" before triggering the customization pipelines.

Architectural Insight:

AFT handles the "Waiting Game." Because account creation is an asynchronous process that can take 30+ minutes, AFT’s state-machine architecture ensures that the Terraform customization phase doesn't start until the account API is fully active.

WEEK 10: DEEP DIVE: AFT
HANDS-ON LAB 10.1

Lab 10.1: The GitOps Request Flow

In an AFT environment, we do not call aws organizations create-account. Instead, we commit a declaration to the Account Requests repository. AFT watches this repo and triggers a Step Function to fulfill the request.

Task: Defining the HCL Request

In your /aft-account-requests directory, create sandbox-account.tf. This file defines the metadata AFT needs to orchestrate the account.

module "sandbox_account_01" { source = "./modules/aft-account-request" control_tower_parameters = { account_email = "devops+aft01@hexadigitall.com" account_name = "hex-aft-sandbox-01" managed_organizational_unit = "Sandbox" sso_user_email = "admin@hexadigitall.com" sso_user_first_name = "Tech" sso_user_last_name = "Poet" } account_tags = { "Project" = "Landed-App" "CostCenter" = "1002" "Provisioner" = "AFT" } }

The Trigger Logic

  • [ ] Validate the HCL syntax using terraform validate.
  • [ ] Simulate a Git Push. In a real AFT setup, this merge to main would trigger the CodePipeline in the AFT Management account.

Architect's Challenge: Why does AFT require the SSO User's First and Last name? Hint: Think about how Control Tower integrates with AWS IAM Identity Center during the account factory process.

WEEK 10: DEEP DIVE: AFT
HANDS-ON LAB 10.2

Lab 10.2: Global Baseline Injection

The aft-global-customizations repository is the engine of standardization. Every resource defined here will be applied to every AFT-vended account. This ensures our Hexadigitall security baseline is never optional.

Task: Defining the Global Baseline

In /aft-global-customizations/terraform/main.tf, define an account-level baseline that locks down sensitive configurations.

# Enforce S3 Public Access Block at the account level resource "aws_s3_account_public_access_block" "global_block" { block_public_acls = true block_public_policy = true ignore_public_acls = true restrict_public_buckets = true } # Create a local 'HexaAdmin' role for break-glass scenarios resource "aws_iam_role" "hexa_breakglass" { name = "HexaBreakGlassAdmin" assume_role_policy = data.aws_iam_policy_document.trust_mgmt.json }

The Orchestration Verification

  • [ ] Draft the api_helpers.py (optional) if you need custom Python logic to run alongside Terraform.
  • [ ] Map out the Execution Order: Account Vending → Global Customizations → Account Specific Customizations.

Architect's Note: AFT Global Customizations are applied using a Terraform backend that lives in the AFT Management account. This means you don't need to manage state files for individual vended accounts; AFT handles the mapping for you.

WEEK 10: DEEP DIVE: AFT
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 10: Deploying Global Customizations via AFT

The objective of this project is to implement a Multi-Account Security Baseline that is automatically applied to every account vended through AFT. You will transition from "One-by-One" account setup to "Fleet-Wide" governance.

Project Deliverables

1. The Global Security DNA

Update the aft-global-customizations repository to include a mandatory AWS Config recorder and an IAM Access Analyzer instance in every region. These resources must be deployed automatically upon account provisioning.

2. The Account-Specific Override

Configure the aft-account-customizations repo to detect an account tagged with Workload: Payment and automatically provision a dedicated CloudHSM cluster or a KMS Key with specific regulatory policies.

3. The AFT Health Dashboard

Create a PROVISIONING_FLOW.md that documents the status of the AFT Step Functions. Capture a successful execution trace that shows the transition from "Account Created" to "Global Customization Complete."

Success Criteria

Standardization Are the security resources present in a newly vended account without manual intervention?
Pipeline Integrity Does a failure in customization trigger an AFT notification/alert?
GitOps Traceability Can you link every resource in the Spoke account back to a specific commit in Git?

Architect's Final Thought: Automation is not about doing things faster; it is about doing things correctly every single time. With AFT, you have moved Hexadigitall from a craft-shop to a factory.

Week 11

Standardization, Blueprints & Service Catalog Onboarding

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Policy-Driven Governance in Multi-Cloud.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: Azure Blueprints vs. AWS Service Catalog.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Architecting an Azure Landing Zone Template.
  • 4. Weekly Project: The "Service-in-a-Box" Catalog Deployment.
  • Enterprise Extension: Workload Onboarding
    Build a workload onboarding playbook that guides teams from blueprint selection through deployment verification. Document governance hand-off, compliance validation checklists, and portfolio artifact requirements for each catalog service.

As **Hexadigitall** scales, clients will demand cloud-agnostic excellence. This week, we pivot to the Microsoft ecosystem to master **Azure Blueprints**. We will explore how to bundle resource groups, policies, role assignments, and ARM templates into a single, repeatable "package." We also dive into the **Service Catalog**—the portal that allows developers to self-provision pre-approved, compliant infrastructure without needing administrative privileges.

"Consistency is the companion of authority. When infrastructure is standardized, security becomes a silent partner rather than a noisy obstacle."
WEEK 11: STANDARDIZATION & BLUEPRINTS
FOUNDATION

Chapter 11: Policy-Driven Governance

In a multi-cloud enterprise like Hexadigitall Technologies, the definition of "compliance" must remain constant, even as the underlying API changes. Policy-Driven Governance is the practice of codifying organizational rules (e.g., "all data must be encrypted," "no public buckets") into machine-readable policy engines that prevent non-compliant resources from ever being created.

The Hierarchy of Standardized Environments

To achieve "Standardization-as-Code," we leverage the Azure Management Hierarchy:

  • P1. Management Groups: The equivalent of AWS OUs. Policies assigned here are inherited by all child subscriptions, ensuring global guardrails.
  • P2. Azure Policy (Guardrails): Real-time enforcement. Unlike SCPs which only "Deny," Azure Policy can "Audit," "Deny," or even "DeployIfNotExist" to automatically remediate drift.
  • P3. Blueprints (The Template): A container for resource groups, role assignments, and policy definitions. It ensures that a new environment isn't just "governed," but fully "composed" of the right parts.

Pre-Class Exercises: Mapping Azure to AWS

  • Ex 1. Comparison Table: Create /week-11-standardization/CLOUD_MAPPING.md. List AWS services (Control Tower, SCP, AVM) and their Azure equivalents.
  • Ex 2. Policy Drafting: Write a pseudo-code policy that denies the creation of any storage account if supportsHttpsTrafficOnly is set to false.
  • Ex 3. Hierarchy Design: Sketch a Management Group structure for a company with "Finance" and "Engineering" departments, each having "Dev" and "Prod" subscriptions.

Architect's Note: In Azure, governance is a "First-Class Citizen." While AWS layers governance on top of accounts, Azure builds it into the fabric of the Management Group hierarchy from the ground up.

WEEK 11: STANDARDIZATION & BLUEPRINTS
TEACHING NOTES

Azure Blueprints vs. AWS Service Catalog

Standardization is about control without the bottleneck. We achieve this by moving from "Tickets" to "Self-Service," using templates that have security and compliance baked in from the start.

1. Azure Blueprints (The Orchestrator)

Azure Blueprints allows you to define a repeatable set of Azure resources that implements and adheres to an organization's standards.

  • • Philosophy: Environment-focused. It creates the "Subscription Baseline."
  • • Locking: Features "Resource Locking" to prevent even administrators from deleting critical governance infrastructure.

2. AWS Service Catalog (The Vending Machine)

Service Catalog allows organizations to create and manage catalogs of IT services that are approved for use on AWS.

  • • Philosophy: Component-focused. It creates the "Application Lego."
  • • Permissions: Uses "Launch Constraints" to allow users to deploy resources they wouldn't normally have IAM permissions to create.

The Architect's Synthesis:

For Hexadigitall, we use Blueprints to set up the foundation of an Azure Subscription (Networking, RBAC, Policies). We use Service Catalog in AWS to give developers a "Menu" of pre-hardened databases and compute clusters that comply with our Phase II security rules.

WEEK 11: STANDARDIZATION & BLUEPRINTS
HANDS-ON LAB 11.1

Lab 11.1: The Azure Hierarchy

Azure governance starts at the Management Group level. We will create a hierarchy that separates "Production" from "Sandbox" and assigns a policy to ensure all data is kept within a specific geographic region.

Task: Defining the Bicep Blueprint

In your /week-11-standardization/azure-baseline directory, create hierarchy.bicep. This script builds the organizational containers.

targetScope = 'tenant' resource hexadigitallMG 'Microsoft.Management/managementGroups@2021-04-01' = { name: 'hex-org' properties: { displayName: 'Hexadigitall Technologies' } } resource productionMG 'Microsoft.Management/managementGroups@2021-04-01' = { name: 'hex-prod' parent: hexadigitallMG properties: { displayName: 'Hexa-Production' } }

The Deployment Command

  • [ ] Login via az login and ensure you have 'Owner' rights at the Tenant Root.
  • [ ] Run az deployment tenant create --location EastUS --template-file hierarchy.bicep.
  • [ ] Verify in the Azure Portal that the hierarchy is reflected under 'Management Groups'.

Architect's Challenge: Why did we set the targetScope to tenant instead of subscription? Hint: Think about where Management Groups "live" in the Azure resource model.

WEEK 11: STANDARDIZATION & BLUEPRINTS
HANDS-ON LAB 11.2

Lab 11.2: The Standardized Spoke VNET

Connectivity must be consistent. This lab creates a "Network Blueprint" that ensures every new Azure Spoke follows the Hexadigitall security posture for traffic isolation.

Task: Implementing the Networking Logic

resource vnet 'Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks@2021-05-01' = { name: 'hex-spoke-vnet' location: resourceGroup().location properties: { addressSpace: { addressPrefixes: [ '10.1.0.0/16' ] } subnets: [ { name: 'WebSubnet' properties: { addressPrefix: '10.1.1.0/24' } } { name: 'AppSubnet' properties: { addressPrefix: '10.1.2.0/24' } } ] } }

Verification Steps

  • [ ] Deploy the Bicep template into a dedicated Resource Group in the 'Sandbox' subscription.
  • [ ] Check the 'Effective Security Rules' on the subnets to ensure the default "Deny-All" inbound from the Internet is active.

Architect's Note: By standardizing the VNET structure, you simplify the "Phase IV" operations where you will need to monitor traffic across hundreds of spokes. If the subnets are named the same everywhere, the scripts are easier to write.

WEEK 11: STANDARDIZATION & BLUEPRINTS
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 11: The "Service-in-a-Box" Catalog

The objective of this project is to implement a Standardized Product Portfolio. You will package a complex architecture—such as a three-tier web app with a hardened database—into a single, clickable product in the AWS Service Catalog or Azure Managed Applications portal.

Project Deliverables

1. The Hardened Product Template

Create a CloudFormation or ARM template for a "Hexa-Standard Database." This product must automatically enable encryption, multi-AZ failover, and restricted security group ingress from the 'AppSubnet' only.

2. Launch Constraint Orchestration

Configure a Launch Role that allows the Service Catalog to provision resources on behalf of a developer who lacks direct 'CreateResource' permissions. This is the cornerstone of "least-privilege" self-service.

3. The Self-Service Portal Guide

Draft a CATALOG_INVENTORY.md that lists the available "Standard Products." For each, include the cost-per-hour estimate and the specific Phase II security rules it satisfies.

Success Criteria

Zero-Privilege Deployment Can a 'Read-Only' user successfully deploy the product via the Catalog?
Compliance Verification Do the provisioned resources trigger any GuardDuty or Azure Policy alerts?
Cost Accountability Are all catalog-provisioned resources tagged with the correct user metadata?

Architect's Insight: You have now completed Phase III. You've built the factory, the blueprints, and finally, the shop front. Your Landing Zone is no longer a static project; it is a living service.

PHASE III ASSESSMENT
AUTOMATION, DELIVERY & PLATFORM GOVERNANCE

Phase III Knowledge Assessment

📋 Assessment Overview

Questions: 18 multiple-choice questions

Time Estimate: 50-70 minutes

Passing Score: 13/18 (72%)

Coverage: Weeks 8-11 (AFT, Terraform, Service Catalog, Drift Management)

1. What is the primary purpose of AWS Control Tower Account Factory for Terraform (AFT)?

  • A) To manage IAM users across accounts
  • B) To provide automated account provisioning and customization
  • C) To monitor resource usage and costs
  • D) To create network connectivity between accounts
Answer: B. AFT automates the entire account lifecycle from creation through customization and governance.

2. Which AFT repository is responsible for applying account-specific customizations?

  • A) aft-global-customizations
  • B) aft-account-customizations
  • C) aft-account-requests
  • D) aft-management
Answer: B. aft-account-customizations contains Terraform code specific to individual accounts.

3. What is "Infrastructure Drift" in the context of Terraform?

  • A) Network latency between regions
  • B) Manual changes to resources not managed by Terraform
  • C) Automatic scaling of resources
  • D) Migration of resources between accounts
Answer: B. Drift occurs when resources are modified outside of Terraform's control, breaking the desired state.

4. Which AWS service allows users to deploy pre-approved infrastructure without IAM permissions?

  • A) AWS Service Catalog
  • B) AWS Config
  • C) AWS Systems Manager
  • D) AWS Organizations
Answer: A. Service Catalog provides a curated portfolio of approved products that users can deploy through a self-service interface.

5. What is the role of "Launch Constraints" in AWS Service Catalog?

  • A) To limit the number of products that can be launched
  • B) To specify which IAM role performs the actual resource creation
  • C) To define cost limits for product deployments
  • D) To restrict product launches to specific regions
Answer: B. Launch constraints specify the IAM role that Service Catalog assumes to create resources on behalf of the user.

6. Which Terraform feature helps prevent multiple engineers from applying changes simultaneously?

  • A) Terraform workspaces
  • B) State locking with DynamoDB
  • C) Remote state
  • D) Terraform Cloud
Answer: B. DynamoDB provides state locking to prevent concurrent modifications that could corrupt the state file.

7. What is the primary benefit of using AWS Config Rules for drift detection?

  • A) They automatically remediate non-compliant resources
  • B) They provide continuous monitoring of resource configurations
  • C) They manage IAM permissions
  • D) They create backups of resources
Answer: B. Config Rules continuously evaluate resource configurations against desired states.

8. Which AFT component manages the account provisioning pipeline?

  • A) AWS CodePipeline
  • B) AWS Step Functions
  • C) AWS Lambda
  • D) AWS EventBridge
Answer: B. Step Functions orchestrates the complex workflow of account creation and customization.

9. What is a "Product" in AWS Service Catalog terminology?

  • A) A pricing tier
  • B) A CloudFormation template or Terraform configuration
  • C) A user permission level
  • D) A compliance requirement
Answer: B. Products are the infrastructure templates that users can deploy through the catalog.

10. Which repository triggers the creation of new accounts in AFT?

  • A) aft-global-customizations
  • B) aft-account-requests
  • C) aft-account-provisioning-customizations
  • D) aft-management
Answer: B. aft-account-requests contains the account creation requests that initiate the AFT pipeline.

11. What is the purpose of "Portfolio" in AWS Service Catalog?

  • A) To track costs
  • B) To group and share related products with specific users
  • C) To define network architectures
  • D) To manage compliance rules
Answer: B. Portfolios organize products and control which users or groups can access them.

12. Which AWS service can automatically remediate non-compliant resources?

  • A) AWS Config
  • B) AWS Systems Manager
  • C) AWS Lambda
  • D) AWS Step Functions
Answer: B. Systems Manager Automation documents can be triggered by Config Rules to remediate issues.

13. What is "GitOps" in the context of Landing Zone management?

  • A) Using Git for version control only
  • B) Treating Git as the source of truth for infrastructure state
  • C) Managing user permissions through Git
  • D) Storing logs in Git repositories
Answer: B. GitOps uses Git repositories as the declarative source of truth for infrastructure.

14. Which AFT repository contains customizations applied to all accounts?

  • A) aft-account-requests
  • B) aft-global-customizations
  • C) aft-account-customizations
  • D) aft-management
Answer: B. aft-global-customizations applies baseline configurations to every account in the organization.

15. What is the benefit of using Terraform modules in a Landing Zone?

  • A) They reduce code duplication
  • B) They automatically scale resources
  • C) They manage user permissions
  • D) They create backups
Answer: A. Modules promote reusability and consistency across different parts of the Landing Zone.

16. Which service provides "Continuous Compliance" monitoring in a Landing Zone?

  • A) AWS Trusted Advisor
  • B) AWS Config
  • C) AWS Cost Explorer
  • D) AWS Personal Health Dashboard
Answer: B. AWS Config provides continuous evaluation of resource compliance against defined rules.

17. What is the role of "State Management" in Terraform?

  • A) Managing user sessions
  • B) Tracking the current state of infrastructure
  • C) Controlling access to resources
  • D) Monitoring performance metrics
Answer: B. Terraform state tracks what resources exist and their current configuration.

18. Which AWS service enables self-service infrastructure deployment with governance?

  • A) AWS Control Tower
  • B) AWS Service Catalog
  • C) AWS Organizations
  • D) AWS Resource Groups
Answer: B. Service Catalog provides governed self-service access to approved infrastructure patterns.

🎯 Phase III Completion

Congratulations on completing Phase III! Your Landing Zone now has automated delivery and governance.

Proceed to Phase IV for lifecycle management and capstone delivery. Your mentor will review your automation pipelines before advancing.

Phase III Assessment
Phase IV

Lifecycle &
Capstone

The final frontier of the Landing Zone Specialist. In this phase, we master the art of maintaining a "Day 2" environment—detecting configuration drift, managing the lifecycle of retired accounts, and ensuring the platform evolves without breaking. Everything leads to the Capstone: The complete Hexadigitall Reference Architecture.

"Architecture is not a destination; it is a persistent state of alignment. The best Landing Zones are not those that never change, but those that change with intention."
Week 12

Compliance Validation, Audit Evidence & Lifecycle Guardrails

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Detecting Configuration Drift at Scale.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: Account Decommissioning & Data Retention.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Automating Drift Remediation with AWS Config.
  • 4. Weekly Project: The "Landing Zone Health" Dashboard.
  • Enterprise Extension: Compliance Validation & Audit Evidence
    Build control evidence packages that demonstrate organizational policy adherence. Create audit readiness checklists (SOC 2, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS) and document how your landing zone architecture meets regulatory requirements.

The masterpiece is finished, but now it must live. This week, we address the reality of "Day 2" operations. We will build mechanisms to detect when an administrator bypasses our automation to make manual changes—known as Drift. We also establish the protocols for the end of the line: how to safely decommission accounts, scrub data, and release resources back to the organization without leaving security holes behind.

"A building that cannot be maintained is a ruin in waiting. A Landing Zone that cannot handle drift is a security incident in progress."
WEEK 12: COMPLIANCE VALIDATION, AUDIT EVIDENCE & LIFECYCLE GUARDRAILS
FOUNDATION

Chapter 12: Detecting Configuration Drift

In a perfect world, all changes to a Landing Zone go through a Pull Request. In the real world, emergency hotfixes, manual console overrides, and "shadow IT" create Configuration Drift. Left unmanaged, drift weakens your security posture and breaks your automation pipelines.

The Mechanics of Drift Awareness

At Hexadigitall, we view drift detection as a continuous audit loop comprising three distinct layers:

  • D1. Terraform State Reconciliation: Using CI/CD schedules to run terraform plan periodically. If the plan isn't empty, drift exists.
  • D2. Resource-Level Compliance: Leveraging AWS Config or Azure Policy to monitor individual resource properties (e.g., an S3 bucket becoming public) in real-time.
  • D3. Management-Level Drift: Detecting when a member account is moved to a different OU or when a Service Control Policy (SCP) is detached by a compromised Management-account user.

Pre-Class Exercises: Identifying the Gap

  • Ex 1. Manual Override Test: Manually change a tag on a resource in your Sandbox account via the Console. Run terraform plan and capture how Terraform identifies this as drift.
  • Ex 2. Config Rule Research: Identify three "AWS Config Managed Rules" that directly correspond to the security baselines we built in Phase II.
  • Ex 3. Drift Severity Matrix: Create DRIFT_SEVERITY.md. Categorize drift (e.g., "Missing Tag" = Low, "Public S3" = Critical) and define response times for each.

Architect's Note: Drift is not a failure of the engineer; it is a feature of the cloud. The goal isn't to prevent 100% of drift—it's to ensure that 100% of drift is visible and manageable.

WEEK 12: COMPLIANCE VALIDATION, AUDIT EVIDENCE & LIFECYCLE GUARDRAILS
TEACHING NOTES

Decommissioning & Data Retention

Closing an account is more than clicking a button. At the enterprise scale of Hexadigitall, it involves a multi-step orchestration to protect intellectual property and satisfy compliance audits.

1. The Offboarding Protocol

Before an account is formally closed in AWS Organizations, the following "Deep Clean" must occur:

  • • Resource Purge: Deleting all non-storage resources (EC2, RDS, Lambda) to stop billing immediately.
  • • Final Snapshot: Moving critical database snapshots and S3 data to the Log Archive or a dedicated "Cold Storage" account.
  • • Network Detachment: Removing Transit Gateway attachments and Peering connections to prevent traffic from hitting a void.

2. The Retention "Black Hole"

Legal requirements often mandate that even if an account is closed, its logs and primary data must exist for years.

  • • S3 Glacier Deep Archive: The destination for retired account data. Cost-effective for 7-10 year retention.
  • • Immutable Backups: Ensuring the final state of the account is captured in an AWS Backup vault with "Vault Lock" enabled.

Operational Risk Warning:

Never close a Management Account or an account that owns a Service Quota shared with the org. Always identify cross-account dependencies (KMS keys, Shared Subnets) before pulling the plug.

WEEK 12: DRIFT & LIFECYCLE
HANDS-ON LAB 12.1

Lab 12.1: Defining the Detective Rule

We will use Terraform to deploy an AWS Config Managed Rule. This rule will act as our "eyes" in the Spoke account, flagging any S3 bucket that does not have server-side encryption enabled.

Task: Deploying the Config Rule

resource "aws_config_config_rule" "s3_encryption_check" { name = "s3-bucket-server-side-encryption-enabled" source { owner = "AWS" source_identifier = "S3_BUCKET_SERVER_SIDE_ENCRYPTION_ENABLED" } depends_on = [aws_config_configuration_recorder.main] }

Verification Steps

  • [ ] Create an unencrypted S3 bucket in the Console named hex-drift-test-bucket.
  • [ ] Navigate to the AWS Config dashboard and wait for the Non-compliant status to appear.
WEEK 12: DRIFT & LIFECYCLE
HANDS-ON LAB 12.2

Lab 12.2: Closing the Remediation Loop

We will now configure Active Remediation. This ensures that even if an administrator manually disables encryption, the Landing Zone will "self-heal" within minutes.

Task: Configuring the Remediation Action

resource "aws_config_remediation_configuration" "s3_auto_fix" { config_rule_name = aws_config_config_rule.s3_encryption_check.name resource_type = "AWS::S3::Bucket" target_type = "SSM_DOCUMENT" target_id = "AWS-ConfigureS3BucketEncryption" parameter { name = "BucketName" resource_value = "RESOURCE_ID" } parameter { name = "SSEAlgorithm" static_value = "AES256" } }

The Final "Drift" Test

  • [ ] Attempt to disable encryption on hex-drift-test-bucket.
  • [ ] Monitor the SSM Automation Execution console.
  • [ ] Verify the bucket encryption is re-enabled automatically without manual intervention.

Architect's Note: While auto-remediation is powerful, use it carefully. Automatically deleting a non-compliant resource (like an RDS instance) could cause data loss. Always prefer Remediation by Configuration (fixing the setting) over Remediation by Destruction.

WEEK 12: DRIFT & LIFECYCLE
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 12: The "Landing Zone Health" Dashboard

The objective of this project is to aggregate disparate security and operational data into a Centralized Health Monitor. You will use Amazon CloudWatch and AWS Config Aggregators to provide a real-time view of your environment's posture.

Project Deliverables

1. The Config Aggregator

Deploy an AWS Config Multi-Account Aggregator in your Audit account. It must pull compliance data from all Sandbox and Production accounts to show a single "Compliance Score."

2. The Drift Alarm System

Configure a CloudWatch Metric Filter that triggers an SNS notification to the devops@hexadigitall.com alias whenever an SSM Auto-Remediation task is executed.

3. The Decommissioning Script

Draft a PURGE_SPOKE.py script (or Boto3 equivalent) that lists all "vulnerable" resources in an account before it is closed, serving as a pre-flight checklist for the architect.

Success Criteria

Centralized View Can you see the compliance status of 'Account B' while logged into 'Audit Account'?
Drift Alerting Does a manual change trigger an email alert within 15 minutes?
Data Traceability Is the dashboard data queryable via Athena for long-term audit reports?

Architect's Final Instruction: You have built a fortress. Now, make sure you can see the walls. This dashboard is the final piece of the Landed App infrastructure puzzle.

Week 13

State Management, Scale Strategy & Resilience Planning

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: Orchestrating State for 100+ Account Estates.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: Workspace vs. Per-Account State Backends.
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Migrating to a High-Performance S3/DynamoDB Backend.
  • 4. Weekly Project: The "State-of-the-Union" Auditor.
  • Enterprise Extension: Operational Runbooks & Incident Response
    Beyond detecting drift, operationalize how teams respond. Create incident response runbooks for different drift scenarios (manual console change, unauthorized policy, missing resource). Document post-incident reviews and process improvements to prevent recurrence.
  • Enterprise Extension: Disaster Recovery & Resilience Planning
    Design recovery objectives (RTO/RPO) for your landing zone architecture. Document backup strategies, failover procedures, regional resilience patterns, and how to reconstruct a landing zone in a disaster scenario using IaC.

As the Hexadigitall environment expands, the "Blast Radius" of a corrupted state file becomes catastrophic. This week, we transition from simple automation to Enterprise Scaling. We focus on partitioning Terraform state to improve performance, utilizing Terragrunt for DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) configurations, and establishing the concurrency controls necessary for a global team to manage hundreds of accounts without collision.

"Scale is not just about having more; it is about how you handle the complexity of 'more'. In a 100-account estate, your state management is your heartbeat."
WEEK 13: STATE MANAGEMENT, SCALE STRATEGY & RESILIENCE PLANNING
FOUNDATION

Chapter 13: Orchestrating State at Scale

As Hexadigitall Technologies scales beyond 100 accounts, the "Blast Radius" of infrastructure changes becomes the primary concern. Scaling strategies focus on State Isolation: the practice of breaking down a massive infrastructure state into smaller, decoupled units that can be updated independently.

The Scaling Paradox: Speed vs. Safety

To manage 100+ accounts efficiently, we implement a Layered State Architecture:

  • S1. Core Layer: Contains the Organization's root structure and TGW Hub. This state is extremely stable and rarely changes.
  • S2. Governance Layer: Manages SCPs, Config Aggregators, and IAM Identity Center. Changes here affect all accounts.
  • S3. Account Layer: A unique state file for every single account vended. If Account 104 has a state corruption, Account 105 remains fully operational.

Pre-Class Exercises: Designing for Density

  • Ex 1. S3 Backend Stress Test: Research the maximum object size for S3 and compare it to a typical terraform.tfstate for a VPC with 50 subnets.
  • Ex 2. Dependency Mapping: Draw a graph showing how an 'Account' state file references the 'Network Hub' state file using terraform_remote_state.
  • Ex 3. Concurrency Audit: Define a DynamoDB Locking strategy that prevents two automated AFT pipelines from modifying the same account simultaneously.

Architect's Warning: At this scale, "Terraform Plan" times become a performance metric. If your plan takes 10 minutes to run, your CI/CD pipeline will fail under the weight of 100 accounts. Partitioning is the only solution.

WEEK 13: STATE MANAGEMENT & SCALING
TEACHING NOTES

Workspaces vs. Per-Account Backends

The core challenge of managing 100+ accounts is State Collision. If multiple automated pipelines attempt to update different accounts that share a single state file or backend, the "Locking" mechanism will become a bottleneck.

1. The Workspace Approach (Centralized)

Workspaces allow you to use one set of configuration files to manage multiple distinct sets of infrastructure resources.

  • • Pros: Dry code; one single terraform init for all accounts.
  • • Cons: High risk. A "State rm" or "State push" error can accidentally target the wrong workspace. Performance degrades as the number of workspaces grows.

2. Isolated Backends (The Hexa-Standard)

In this model, every account (or small group of accounts) has its own S3 Key and DynamoDB Lock.

  • • Pros: Maximum blast-radius protection. Parallel execution across 100 accounts is physically isolated.
  • • Cons: Requires more complex automation (e.g., AFT or Terragrunt) to generate backend configurations dynamically.

The Architect's Verdict:

For Hexadigitall, we utilize Isolated Backends. While Workspaces are excellent for Dev/Stage/Prod within a single application, they lack the administrative separation required for a 100-account corporate Landing Zone.

WEEK 13: STATE MANAGEMENT & SCALING
HANDS-ON LAB 13.1

Lab 13.1: Building the Isolated State Vault

We will provision the backend infrastructure in the Management or Security account to store the state of our 100+ spoke accounts.

Task: Defining the Backend Resources

# DynamoDB for State Locking resource "aws_dynamodb_table" "state_lock" { name = "hex-spoke-101-lock" billing_mode = "PAY_PER_REQUEST" hash_key = "LockID" attribute { name = "LockID" type = "S" } } # S3 for State Storage resource "aws_s3_bucket" "state_storage" { bucket = "hex-spoke-101-state-vault" lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } }

Success Criteria

  • [ ] S3 Bucket must have Versioning Enabled to allow for state recovery.
  • [ ] Public Access Block must be applied to the S3 bucket to prevent state exposure.
WEEK 13: STATE MANAGEMENT & SCALING
HANDS-ON LAB 13.2

Lab 13.2: The Migration Command

Transitioning state is a critical skill. You must ensure that no resources are duplicated or destroyed during the move.

Task: Initializing the Migration

1. Update your backend.tf with the new S3 and DynamoDB details from Lab 13.1.

terraform { backend "s3" { bucket = "hex-spoke-101-state-vault" key = "infrastructure/v1.tfstate" region = "us-east-1" dynamodb_table = "hex-spoke-101-lock" encrypt = true } }

2. Execute the migration in your terminal:

$ terraform init -migrate-state

Verification Checklist

  • [ ] Did Terraform prompt you to "copy the existing state to the new backend"? (Answer 'Yes').
  • [ ] Check the S3 bucket: Is the v1.tfstate file present?
  • [ ] Run terraform plan. Does it show "No changes"? (This confirms a successful migration).

Architect's Tip: In a 100-account environment, you would never do this manually. You would use Terragrunt or AFT to generate these backend.tf blocks dynamically as part of the account vending process.

WEEK 13: STATE MANAGEMENT & SCALING
WEEKLY PROJECT

Project 13: The "State-of-the-Union" Auditor

The objective of this project is to create a State Sentinel. You will build a system that alerts the platform team if any state file in the 100-account estate is accessed manually (outside of CI/CD), if a lock is held for more than 4 hours (indicating a hung pipeline), or if versioning is disabled.

Project Deliverables

1. The Access Sentinel

Implement S3 Bucket Notifications on your State Vault. Trigger a Lambda function whenever a .tfstate file is read. If the IAM Principal is not the 'AFT-Execution-Role', fire a High-Severity alert to the Security Ops channel.

2. The Stale Lock Detector

Create a Python script (run via EventBridge/Lambda) that scans the DynamoDB State Lock table. Any item with a Created timestamp older than 4 hours must be flagged for manual inspection to prevent pipeline deadlocks.

3. The Integrity Report

Generate a STATE_HEALTH.md report that lists all 100+ state keys and confirms that MFA-Delete and Object-Lock are active on each, ensuring that not even a compromised admin can wipe the environment history.

Success Criteria

Detection Latency Does a manual 'S3 Get' on a state file trigger an alert within 60 seconds?
Pipeline Recovery Can the auditor identify a "Zombie" lock without disrupting active runs?
Immutability Proof Can you provide a version history for a specific Spoke's state across three deployments?

Architect's Final Note: In the world of Landing Zones, your state is your memory. If you lose your memory, you lose your environment. Treat your .tfstate files with more reverence than your production databases.

Week 14

Capstone Delivery, Executive Handover & Architecture Defense

Weekly Technical Scope
  • 1. Foundation: The Pillars of Operational Readiness.
  • 2. Teaching Notes: Documentation as Infrastructure (DaI).
  • 3. Hands-on Lab: Generating Automated Architecture Diagrams.
  • 4. Capstone Project: The Full Hexadigitall Environment Sign-off.

The building is built, the security is armed, and the automation is humming. Now comes the most critical part: The Handover. This week is about professionalizing the Landing Zone. We focus on Runbooks, Disaster Recovery validation, and Cost Optimization reporting. You will prove that the environment is not just "ready to use," but ready to be managed by a Tier-1 Operations team without your constant intervention.

"A true specialist is measured by how well their system runs when they are no longer in the room. Documentation is the bridge between a project and a legacy."
WEEK 14: THE CORPORATE HANDOVER
FOUNDATION

Chapter 14: The Pillars of Operational Readiness

A Landing Zone is not "finished" until the operations team can maintain it without the original architect. At Hexadigitall Technologies, we measure readiness through four pillars that transform a technical project into a stable business asset.

The 4 Pillars of the Handover

To achieve "Operational Excellence," every specialist must deliver the following:

  • R1. Incident Response Playbooks: Clear, step-by-step instructions for common issues (e.g., "What to do if a Transit Gateway attachment fails").
  • R2. Configuration Baselines: A "Known-Good" snapshot of all 100+ account settings, enabling the team to identify drift instantly.
  • R3. Financial Guardrails: Automated reports that link cloud costs back to specific business units (Cost Centers) defined in Phase I.
  • R4. Security Ownership: A formal "Transfer of Keys" where root access is rotated and MFA is handed over to the permanent Security Operations Center (SOC).

Pre-Class Exercises: The Checklist

  • Ex 1. The Break-Glass Audit: Document the exact physical or digital location of the Root MFA device for the Management Account. Verify who has access.
  • Ex 2. SLA Mapping: Define the "Uptime Expectation" for the Network Hub. If the Hub is down, how many business-critical applications are affected?
  • Ex 3. Contact Matrix: Create ESCALATION_PATH.md. Who is called when the AFT pipeline fails at 3:00 AM?

Specialist Insight: The mark of a master architect is the silence that follows their departure. If you are still receiving "emergency" calls three months after the handover, the system was not truly ready.

WEEK 14: THE CORPORATE HANDOVER
TEACHING NOTES

Documentation as Infrastructure (DaI)

The greatest threat to a 100-account Landing Zone is Tribal Knowledge. If the logic of the network or the purpose of a specific SCP exists only in the architect's mind, the system is fragile. DaI ensures the "Why" is versioned alongside the "What."

1. The Self-Documenting Codebase

We utilize tools like terraform-docs to automatically generate Markdown files from our HCL variables and outputs.

  • • Standard: Every module must contain a README.md that is auto-updated by the CI/CD pipeline on every merge.
  • • Logic: Comments in code must explain why a decision was made (e.g., "Denying S3:PutBucketPolicy to prevent manual permission bypass") rather than just what the code does.

2. The Architecture Decision Record (ADR)

An ADR is a short text file that captures a significant architectural decision.

  • • Format: Title, Date, Status (Proposed/Accepted), Context, Decision, and Consequences.
  • • Value: When the team changes in two years, the ADR explains why Hexadigitall chose Transit Gateway over VPC Peering.

The Architect's Mantra:

If it isn't documented in the repo, it doesn't exist. If the documentation doesn't match the code, both are wrong.

WEEK 14: THE CORPORATE HANDOVER
HANDS-ON LAB 14.1

Lab 14.1: Mapping the 100-Account Hub

A visual map is the first thing an operations team needs during an incident. We will automate this using Python.

Task: Scripting the Visual

from diagrams import Cluster, Diagram from diagrams.aws.network import TransitGateway, VPC from diagrams.aws.general import Users with Diagram("Hexadigitall Global Hub", show=False): users = Users("Dev Teams") with Cluster("Network Hub Account"): tgw = TransitGateway("Core-TGW") with Cluster("Spoke Accounts (1-100)"): spokes = [VPC(f"Spoke-{i}") for i in range(1, 4)] # Simplified for viz users >> spokes >> tgw

Operational Steps

  • [ ] Install the requirements: pip install diagrams.
  • [ ] Run the script to generate hexadigitall_global_hub.png.
  • [ ] Commit the image and the script to the /docs/arch-diagrams folder in your Git repo.
WEEK 14: THE CORPORATE HANDOVER
HANDS-ON LAB 14.2

Lab 14.2: The Compliance Audit

We will automate the "Final Check" before sign-off.

Task: Running the Cross-Account Tag Scan

Use the AWS CLI with the --resource-type-filters flag to identify any resource missing the Environment tag.

$ aws resourcegroupstaggingapi get-resources \ --tag-filters Key=Environment,Values= \ --region us-east-1

Final Validation

  • [ ] Ensure zero resources are returned (indicating 100% tag compliance).
  • [ ] Export the results to a HANDOVER_AUDIT_MARCH_2026.csv.

Architect's Achievement: You have moved from a builder to a governor. This report is your technical "Proof of Work" that the environment is professional, compliant, and ready for business.

WEEK 14: THE CORPORATE HANDOVER
CAPSTONE PROJECT

Capstone: The Hexadigitall Master Landing Zone

The Capstone Project requires the deployment and formal documentation of a production-ready, multi-account environment. You must prove that Phase I through IV are integrated into a single, cohesive GitOps machine.

Final Deliverables (The Handover Pack)

1. The "Golden" Git Repository

A clean, modularized Terraform/AFT repository containing the Global Baseline, Network Hub, and Security Spoke configurations. Every module must have 100% documentation coverage.

2. The Operational Runbook

A comprehensive RUNBOOK.md defining how to: vend a new account, remediate a drift alert, rotate the TGW encryption keys, and decommission a project safely.

3. The Compliance Evidence Locker

A collection of automated reports (Config Aggregator, Security Hub, and Tag Audits) proving that the 100-account estate satisfies all corporate guardrails.

Final Certification Rubric

Automation Can a new compliant account be vended with zero manual console interaction?
Resilience Is the state management partitioned to prevent a single point of failure?
Clarity Could a new DevOps engineer understand the architecture in under 30 minutes?

End of Curriculum

You have demonstrated the technical mastery and operational discipline required of a Hexadigitall Specialist. The cloud is no longer a wilderness; it is a governed engine.

PHASE IV ASSESSMENT
LIFECYCLE MANAGEMENT & COMPLIANCE

Phase IV Knowledge Assessment

📋 Assessment Overview

Questions: 20 multiple-choice questions

Time Estimate: 60-80 minutes

Passing Score: 14/20 (70%)

Coverage: Weeks 12-14 (Compliance, Lifecycle, Capstone Delivery)

1. What is the primary purpose of AWS Config Rules in a Landing Zone?

  • A) To monitor resource costs
  • B) To enforce compliance and detect configuration drift
  • C) To manage user permissions
  • D) To create backups
Answer: B. Config Rules provide continuous compliance monitoring and drift detection.

2. Which AWS service is best suited for automated remediation of non-compliant resources?

  • A) AWS Config
  • B) AWS Systems Manager Automation
  • C) AWS CloudWatch
  • D) AWS Trusted Advisor
Answer: B. Systems Manager Automation can execute remediation runbooks when Config Rules detect violations.

3. What is "Configuration Drift" in the context of infrastructure management?

  • A) Network latency issues
  • B) Manual changes that deviate from the desired state
  • C) Automatic scaling events
  • D) Resource migration between regions
Answer: B. Drift occurs when resources are modified outside of Infrastructure as Code, breaking compliance.

4. Which service provides immutable log storage in a Landing Zone?

  • A) AWS CloudWatch Logs
  • B) AWS S3 with Object Lock
  • C) AWS RDS
  • D) AWS DynamoDB
Answer: B. S3 Object Lock provides WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage for audit logs.

5. What is the role of "Account Baselines" in Landing Zone management?

  • A) To track resource usage
  • B) To define the approved configuration state for accounts
  • C) To manage billing
  • D) To create user accounts
Answer: B. Baselines establish the "known good" state against which drift is measured.

6. Which AWS service enables cross-account log aggregation?

  • A) AWS Organizations
  • B) AWS CloudTrail Organization Trails
  • C) AWS Config Aggregators
  • D) All of the above
Answer: D. All these services support cross-account aggregation for comprehensive monitoring.

7. What is the primary benefit of "Documentation as Infrastructure" (DaI)?

  • A) Reduces development time
  • B) Ensures operational knowledge transfer
  • C) Lowers infrastructure costs
  • D) Improves code quality
Answer: B. DaI ensures that documentation is maintained alongside code, enabling proper handover.

8. Which service provides compliance evidence for regulatory frameworks?

  • A) AWS Artifact
  • B) AWS Config
  • C) AWS Trusted Advisor
  • D) AWS Cost Explorer
Answer: A. AWS Artifact provides on-demand access to AWS compliance reports and agreements.

9. What is the purpose of "Operational Runbooks" in a Landing Zone?

  • A) To document code changes
  • B) To provide step-by-step incident response procedures
  • C) To track project timelines
  • D) To manage budgets
Answer: B. Runbooks ensure consistent response to common operational issues.

10. Which AWS service enables automated cost optimization?

  • A) AWS Cost Explorer
  • B) AWS Budgets
  • C) AWS Compute Optimizer
  • D) All of the above
Answer: D. Multiple services work together to provide comprehensive cost management.

11. What is "Account Decommissioning" in Landing Zone lifecycle management?

  • A) Deleting unused accounts
  • B) The secure process of retiring accounts while preserving compliance
  • C) Upgrading account tiers
  • D) Migrating accounts between regions
Answer: B. Decommissioning involves secure data removal, resource cleanup, and audit trail preservation.

12. Which service provides continuous security assessment?

  • A) Amazon GuardDuty
  • B) AWS Security Hub
  • C) Amazon Inspector
  • D) All of the above
Answer: D. Multiple services provide comprehensive security monitoring and assessment.

13. What is the benefit of "Immutable Infrastructure"?

  • A) Reduces costs
  • B) Prevents configuration drift and ensures consistency
  • C) Improves performance
  • D) Simplifies development
Answer: B. Immutable infrastructure prevents manual changes and ensures reproducible deployments.

14. Which AWS service provides automated backup management?

  • A) AWS Backup
  • B) AWS Storage Gateway
  • C) Amazon S3
  • D) AWS Snowball
Answer: A. AWS Backup provides centralized backup management across multiple AWS services.

15. What is "Disaster Recovery Testing" in Landing Zone operations?

  • A) Testing backup restoration
  • B) Validating failover procedures and recovery time objectives
  • C) Checking network connectivity
  • D) Monitoring resource usage
Answer: B. DR testing ensures that recovery procedures work and meet business requirements.

16. Which service enables automated compliance reporting?

  • A) AWS Config
  • B) AWS Security Hub
  • C) AWS Audit Manager
  • D) All of the above
Answer: D. Multiple services contribute to comprehensive compliance reporting and evidence collection.

17. What is the purpose of "Change Management" in Landing Zone operations?

  • A) To track code changes
  • B) To ensure changes are approved, tested, and documented
  • C) To manage user permissions
  • D) To monitor performance
Answer: B. Change management ensures that modifications don't introduce risk or break compliance.

18. Which AWS service provides centralized logging for multiple accounts?

  • A) AWS CloudWatch
  • B) AWS CloudTrail
  • C) Amazon S3
  • D) AWS Kinesis
Answer: B. CloudTrail Organization Trails aggregate logs from all accounts in the organization.

19. What is "Operational Readiness" in the context of Landing Zone handover?

  • A) Having all features implemented
  • B) Ensuring operations teams can maintain the environment independently
  • C) Meeting project deadlines
  • D) Achieving cost targets
Answer: B. Operational readiness means the system can be managed without the original architect's involvement.

20. Which service enables automated architecture diagram generation?

  • A) AWS Systems Manager
  • B) AWS Config
  • C) AWS CloudFormation
  • D) Amazon Q
Answer: B. AWS Config can generate architecture diagrams from resource configurations.

🎯 Phase IV Completion

Congratulations on completing Phase IV! Your Landing Zone is now operationally ready.

Proceed to the final comprehensive certification exam to validate your complete mastery of Landing Zone architecture.

Phase IV Assessment
HEXADIGITALL SPECIALIST CERTIFICATION
COMPREHENSIVE FINAL EXAM

Landing Zone Specialist Certification

🎯 Final Assessment Overview

Questions: 25 multiple-choice questions

Time Estimate: 75-90 minutes

Passing Score: 19/25 (76%)

Coverage: All 4 phases + comprehensive integration

1. Which AWS service provides the foundational structure for multi-account governance in a Landing Zone?

  • A) AWS Control Tower
  • B) AWS Organizations
  • C) AWS Resource Access Manager
  • D) AWS Systems Manager
Answer: B. AWS Organizations provides the core structure with SCPs, OUs, and consolidated billing.

2. What is the primary purpose of AWS Control Tower Account Factory for Terraform (AFT)?

  • A) To manage IAM users
  • B) To automate account provisioning and customization
  • C) To monitor resource usage
  • D) To create network connectivity
Answer: B. AFT provides automated, governed account vending with Terraform integration.

3. Which service enables self-service infrastructure deployment with governance?

  • A) AWS Control Tower
  • B) AWS Service Catalog
  • C) AWS Organizations
  • D) AWS Resource Groups
Answer: B. Service Catalog provides governed self-service access to approved infrastructure patterns.

4. What is "Configuration Drift" and how is it detected?

  • A) Network latency between regions
  • B) Manual changes outside IaC, detected by AWS Config
  • C) Automatic scaling events
  • D) Resource migration between accounts
Answer: B. Drift occurs when resources deviate from their defined state, detected by AWS Config Rules.

5. Which AWS service provides immutable log storage for compliance?

  • A) AWS CloudWatch Logs
  • B) Amazon S3 with Object Lock
  • C) AWS RDS
  • D) AWS DynamoDB
Answer: B. S3 Object Lock provides WORM storage for audit trails and compliance evidence.

6. What is the role of "Launch Constraints" in AWS Service Catalog?

  • A) To limit product launches
  • B) To specify the IAM role for resource creation
  • C) To define cost limits
  • D) To restrict regions
Answer: B. Launch constraints define which IAM role Service Catalog assumes to create resources.

7. Which service enables automated remediation of non-compliant resources?

  • A) AWS Config
  • B) AWS Systems Manager Automation
  • C) AWS CloudWatch
  • D) AWS Trusted Advisor
Answer: B. Systems Manager Automation can execute remediation runbooks triggered by Config Rules.

8. What is "GitOps" in Landing Zone management?

  • A) Using Git for version control only
  • B) Treating Git as the source of truth for infrastructure
  • C) Managing permissions through Git
  • D) Storing logs in Git
Answer: B. GitOps uses Git repositories as the declarative source of truth for infrastructure state.

9. Which AWS service provides compliance evidence for regulatory frameworks?

  • A) AWS Artifact
  • B) AWS Config
  • C) AWS Trusted Advisor
  • D) AWS Cost Explorer
Answer: A. AWS Artifact provides on-demand access to compliance reports and certifications.

10. What is the benefit of "Immutable Infrastructure"?

  • A) Reduces costs
  • B) Prevents configuration drift and ensures consistency
  • C) Improves performance
  • D) Simplifies development
Answer: B. Immutable infrastructure prevents manual changes and ensures reproducible deployments.

11. Which service enables cross-account log aggregation?

  • A) AWS Organizations
  • B) AWS CloudTrail Organization Trails
  • C) AWS Config Aggregators
  • D) All of the above
Answer: D. Multiple services support cross-account aggregation for comprehensive monitoring.

12. What is "Operational Readiness" in Landing Zone handover?

  • A) Having all features implemented
  • B) Ensuring operations teams can maintain the environment independently
  • C) Meeting project deadlines
  • D) Achieving cost targets
Answer: B. Operational readiness means the system can be managed without the original architect.

13. Which AWS service provides automated cost optimization?

  • A) AWS Cost Explorer
  • B) AWS Budgets
  • C) AWS Compute Optimizer
  • D) All of the above
Answer: D. Multiple services work together to provide comprehensive cost management and optimization.

14. What is the purpose of "Account Baselines"?

  • A) To track resource usage
  • B) To define approved configuration states
  • C) To manage billing
  • D) To create user accounts
Answer: B. Baselines establish the "known good" state against which drift is measured.

15. Which service provides continuous security assessment?

  • A) Amazon GuardDuty
  • B) AWS Security Hub
  • C) Amazon Inspector
  • D) All of the above
Answer: D. Multiple services provide comprehensive security monitoring and threat detection.

16. What is "Documentation as Infrastructure" (DaI)?

  • A) Storing docs in infrastructure
  • B) Maintaining documentation alongside code
  • C) Using docs to build infrastructure
  • D) Infrastructure that generates docs
Answer: B. DaI ensures operational knowledge is maintained and transferred alongside code changes.

17. Which AWS service enables automated backup management?

  • A) AWS Backup
  • B) AWS Storage Gateway
  • C) Amazon S3
  • D) AWS Snowball
Answer: A. AWS Backup provides centralized backup management across multiple AWS services.

18. What is "Change Management" in Landing Zone operations?

  • A) Tracking code changes
  • B) Ensuring approved, tested, documented modifications
  • C) Managing user permissions
  • D) Monitoring performance
Answer: B. Change management ensures modifications don't introduce risk or break compliance.

19. Which service enables automated compliance reporting?

  • A) AWS Config
  • B) AWS Security Hub
  • C) AWS Audit Manager
  • D) All of the above
Answer: D. Multiple services contribute to comprehensive compliance reporting and evidence collection.

20. What is "Account Decommissioning"?

  • A) Deleting unused accounts
  • B) Secure retirement while preserving compliance
  • C) Upgrading account tiers
  • D) Migrating accounts
Answer: B. Decommissioning involves secure data removal, resource cleanup, and audit trail preservation.

21. Which AWS service provides centralized logging for multiple accounts?

  • A) AWS CloudWatch
  • B) AWS CloudTrail
  • C) Amazon S3
  • D) AWS Kinesis
Answer: B. CloudTrail Organization Trails aggregate logs from all accounts in the organization.

22. What is the role of "State Management" in Terraform?

  • A) Managing user sessions
  • B) Tracking infrastructure current state
  • C) Controlling access
  • D) Monitoring performance
Answer: B. Terraform state tracks what resources exist and their current configuration.

23. Which service enables automated architecture diagram generation?

  • A) AWS Systems Manager
  • B) AWS Config
  • C) AWS CloudFormation
  • D) Amazon Q
Answer: B. AWS Config can generate architecture diagrams from resource configurations.

24. What is "Disaster Recovery Testing"?

  • A) Testing backup restoration
  • B) Validating failover procedures and RTOs
  • C) Checking network connectivity
  • D) Monitoring resource usage
Answer: B. DR testing ensures recovery procedures work and meet business requirements.

25. Which principle ensures Landing Zone maintainability?

  • A) Maximum automation
  • B) Infrastructure as Code
  • C) Continuous monitoring
  • D) All of the above
Answer: D. All these principles work together to ensure the Landing Zone remains maintainable and compliant.

Final Certification
BACK MATTER: REFERENCE
THE TRANSLATOR (GLOSSARY)

Technical Terms & Definitions

Governance & Structure

AWS Organizations: The foundational service for account consolidation. It enables hierarchical grouping into OUs and centralizes billing/policy enforcement.

Control Tower: An AWS managed service that orchestrates Organizations, SSO, and Config to set up and govern a secure multi-account environment.

OU (Organizational Unit): A logical container for accounts within an organization. Policies (SCPs) applied to an OU are inherited by all child accounts.

SCP (Service Control Policy): A JSON policy that sets the maximum available permissions for an account or OU. It acts as a "filter" and cannot grant permissions, only restrict them.

Blast Radius: The total impact surface area of a technical failure or security breach. Landing Zones use account boundaries to minimize this area.

Automation & State

AFT (Account Factory for Terraform): A GitOps-driven engine that automates account creation and the application of custom Terraform baselines.

Drift: When the "Actual State" of a cloud resource deviates from the "Desired State" defined in HCL code, usually due to manual console changes.

Idempotency: The property where an automation script (like Terraform) produces the exact same result whether run once or one thousand times.

State Locking: A mechanism using DynamoDB to prevent multiple concurrent Terraform executions from writing to the same .tfstate file and causing corruption.

Terragrunt: A thin wrapper for Terraform that provides extra tools for keeping configurations DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) and managing multiple remote states.

Networking & Security

Transit Gateway (TGW): A regional network hub that acts as a cloud router, simplifying VPC peering by connecting thousands of VPCs and on-premises networks.

RAM (Resource Access Manager): A service used to share AWS resources (like TGWs or Subnets) across accounts within an organization.

PrivateLink: A technology that provides private connectivity between VPCs and AWS services without using public IP addresses or traversing the internet.

Guardrails: High-level rules. Preventative guardrails (SCPs) stop actions; Detective guardrails (AWS Config) notify you when a violation has already occurred.

IAM Identity Center (SSO): A centralized service to manage single sign-on access to all AWS accounts and cloud applications from a single Identity Provider.

BACK MATTER: APPENDIX
TROUBLESHOOTING QUICK-REFERENCE

The "Day 2" Recovery Matrix

Domain / Issue Probable Root Cause Standard Remediation
STATE: Lock Error A CI/CD runner crashed or timed out before releasing the DynamoDB lock. Locate LockID in error output. Verify no active process. Run: terraform force-unlock [ID].
AFT: Provisioning Hang Service Quota limit reached in target region (e.g., max VPCs) or Control Tower hang. Check Step Function "Failure" status in AFT-Management. Request Quota increase via Service Quotas console.
IAM: "Entity Already Exists" Manual console changes created a role/policy that Terraform is now trying to create. Manually delete the console resource or use terraform import to bring it under code control.
SCP: Access Denied Conflict between Local IAM "Allow" and a restrictive "Deny" at the OU level. Use IAM Policy Simulator. Select "Simulation with SCPs". Identify the specific blocking policy ID.
TGW: No Connectivity Missing TGW Attachment, or Route Table propagation is not enabled for the spoke. Verify Spoke VPC Route Table points 0.0.0.0/0 to TGW. Verify TGW Route Table has an active propagation from the spoke.
DNS: Resolution Failure Route 53 Resolver Rules not shared via RAM or VPC not associated with the Hub zone. Check RAM shares in Spoke. Verify "VPC Association" in the Route 53 Private Hosted Zone console.
DRIFT: Config Non-Compliant Remediation failed due to insufficient SSM permissions or resource deletion. Check SSM Automation execution logs. Ensure the Config-Remediation-Role has appropriate PUT permissions.

Emergency Protocol:

If an issue cannot be resolved via code, utilize the Break-Glass Role (managed in IAM Identity Center). Document all manual actions in the POST_MORTEM.md repo and trigger a terraform apply immediately after to restore the baseline.
